SourceName: Ameresco Wasatch RNG LLC- Davis Landfill Renewable Natural Gas Facility(Site ID: 16243)
GeneralDescription: Ameresco Wasatch RNG LLC (Ameresco) has requested to construct a Renewable Natural Gas ( RNG) Facility , which will treat landfill gas and deliver the treated gas to a natural gas pipeline distribution system in Layton, Davis County. The landfill gas will come from the adjacent Wasatch Integrated Waste Management District (WIWMD) Davis Landfill. The raw la ndfill gas will be delivered to the feed blowers at the inlet of the new Ameresco facility , where it will be filtered and compressed. The gas then enters a fixed -bed hydrogen sulfide (H 2S) removal unit utilizing activated carbon media before being sent to a temperature or pressure swing adsorption technology (TSA or PSA) system, where VOCs are removed from the gas stream. The conditioned gas will then be sent to a carbon dioxide (CO 2) separation system and PSA nitrogen (N 2) removal unit to remove CO 2 and N2 from the gas. Oxygen (O 2) may also be removed from the gas steam by use of a catalytic oxidation process in a Deoxo system. Gas will then be dehydrated utilizing a TSA system. The TSA will have closed -loop regeneration and therefore will not vent off -gases. The processed gas will then be sent to product compressors , where it will be pressurized to meet pipeline pressure requirements. Through the processes described above, the gas will be filtered, dewatered , and compressed, thus meeting the definition of "treatment system" in Federal Code 40 CFR 60.41(f). The waste gas generated from the above processing and treatment system will be sent to a thermal oxidizer or an open flare. DAQE -AN162430001 -25 Page 4

GeneralDescription: This AO is issued to Ames Construction, Inc orporated for the purpose of operating a portable source that belongs to the nonmetallic mineral processing industry, including aggregate, concrete, and hot mix asphalt plants. The plants subject to this AO shall be temporarily operated for a period of not more than 180 working days at any location. A relocation shall not exceed 365 consecutive days at any location in the State of Utah. Prior to commencement of operation at a location, the owner/operator shall s ubmit a Notice of Temporary Relocation to the Director and obtain a Temporary Relocation Approval Letter. If the owner/operator operates at a location in compliance with the AO, the Notice of Temporary Relocation, and the Temporary Relocation Approval Let ter, dispersion modeling results have determined that there will be no adverse impacts on air quality at the nearest residence or commercial establishment. Compliance with the opacity limits and operating practices contained in the conditions of the AO s hall be considered as application of BACT. The emission control measures required in the conditions of this AO shall apply to all of the locations at which the source approved by this AO operates. SourceName: Autoliv North America-Airbag Inflator Facility(Site ID: 11460)
GeneralDescription: Autoliv North America (Autoliv) manufactures inflators used in automotive air bags at a manufacturing plant in Brigham City. Inflators generate gas to inflate airbags by gas -generating tablets, stored gas, or a combination of both. Tablet loading stations are situated on the manufacturing line with pressure release stacks over the loading stations in the event of an ignition to protect personnel and the facility. The stacks are vented through the ceiling and are only in place in the event of an accidental ignition of tablets. The stored gas inflator lines consist of filling inert gas under pressure into an inflator. When the iner t gas is rapidly released , the air bag will fill with gas and perform as designed. Inert gases are stored in large outside tanks and the gas is delivered in pressurized lines to production locations. The IBC facility performs quality and environmental test ing on inflators as required by the customer. The quality testing area deploys inflators in an enclosed vessel to measure gas pressure and quality. The environmental testing area conditions inflators in cooling and heating chambers to evaluate the effect o f environmental conditions on inflator performance.

SourceName: Balchem Corporation, Inc. - Mineral Feed Supplement Plant(Site ID: 10503)
GeneralDescription: Balchem Corporation , Incorporated (Balchem) manufactures metal -based mineral feed supplements for use in poultry, aquaculture and pet food by combining metallic and non -metallic mineral compounds and organic acid to produce feed proteins such as grain. The resulting complex reaction with these compounds is a " chelate ." Raw m aterials are delivered from storage bins by a screw conveyor and bucket elevators to a batching bin and then transferred by gravity flow to enclosed mixing tanks that are connected in a series. To balance the pH and increase the bioavailability of the fee d stock, an aqueous, dilute acid solution is introduced to the system during mixing. This slurry is transferred via a screw conveyor to a ring dryer. When dried, the product is conveyed to the holding/weighing bins and packaged into various bags, buckets or bulk containers. To control emissions, both of the dryers are equipped with baghouses. The ring dryer is equipped with a cyclone where the initial stream of dried product is collected.

SourceName: Bimbo Bakeries USA, Inc.- Salt Lake City Bakery(Site ID: 10369)
GeneralDescription: Bimbo Bakeries USA, Inc. (BBU) has submitted an NOI , dated December 6, 2016, to install a catalytic oxidizer to reduce potential and actual emissions o f VOC below the major source applicability threshol d. BBU is located in Salt Lake County, which is to be declared a serious nonattainment area for PM 2.5. When this occurs, the major source threshold for PM 2.5 and all PM 2.5 precursor emissions becomes 70 tons per year. Salt Lake County is also a nonattai nment area of the NAAQS for PM 10 , SO 2 and a maintenance area for ozone. NSPS, NESHAP and MACT regulations do not apply to this source. Title V of the 1990 CAA does not apply to this source. The project involves routing the exhaust from all t hree baking ovens through the catalytic oxidizer pr ior to release through a single stack. Some additional emissions from the combustion of natural gas will result, although VOC emissions will decrease. A co rrection will also be made to balance the particula te emissions between PM 10 and PM 2.5 . These emissions changes (in tons per year) are a s follows: PM 10 - 0.33, PM 2.5 +1.17, SO 2 +0.01, NO x +2.28, CO +1.92, VOC -50.00, formaldehyde -0.21 an d benzene - 0.12. GHG emissions were not previously listed, an d are calculated at 17040 tons as CO 2e. These changes result in the following plant-wide PTE (all values in tons per year): PM 10 = 2.62, PM 2.5 = 2.62, SO 2 = 0.08, NO x = 13.75, CO = 11.55, VOC 45.00, formaldehyde = 0.0 1 and benzene = 0.0003. Total HAP emissions are estimated at 0.7 tons/yr. SourceName: Building Materials Manufacturing LLC- Insulation Board and Roofing Membrane Prod. Plant(Site ID: 14603)
GeneralDescription: BMCA Cedar City LLC (BMCA) is the owner and operator of an insulation board and roofing membrane production plant in Cedar City, Iron County. The facility operates thre e separate manufacturing lines for the production of polyisocyanurate (ISO) insulation board, thermoplastic polyolefin (TPO) roofing membranes, and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) roofing membranes. The ISO insulation board production line utilizes methylene di phenyl diisocyanate (MDI), polyol, and a blowing agent (e.g., pentane). The MDI and blended polyol/blowing agent material is transferred onto a bottom facer material. Upon contact, the materials exothermically react, polymerize, and cross -link to rapidly f orm rigid closed -cell foam. The foam board is ultimately transferred to a series of enclosures to be cut to final dimensions. Emissions generated from the ISO foam board production line are captured and routed first through a baghouse for particulate contr ol and then to a regenerative thermal oxidizer (RTO) to control VOCs. The TPO and PVC roofing membrane production lines utilize a pneumatic conveyance system to convey the raw materials to feeder bins that meter the raw materials into the extruders. In t he extruders, polymers and additives are blended and heated. The core extrudate passes through a die and is applied directly to a scrim reinforcement sheet, typically woven polyester material. After application of the core (bottom of the roofing membrane) onto the scrim, the sheet passes through a series of calendar rolls that press the core onto the scrim. The sheet then passes to the cap extruder die , where the cap (top of the roofing membrane) extrudate is applied to the other side of the scrim. Addition al calendar rolls are employed to assist with bonding the cap to the scrim. DAQE -AN146030003 -24 Page 4

The appropriate molds are sprayed with a gel coat in a vented area. The molds are then fi lled with a mixture of catalyzed resin, fillers, and color. The molds are vibrated and then placed on drying racks. The dried material is removed from the mold , and the rough edges are ground off. The product is polished with a buffing compound and sent out to the job site.

SourceName: CMC Rock, LLC - Heber City Concrete Batch Plant(Site ID: 16153)
GeneralDescription: CMC Rock, LLC (CMC Rock) has proposed a new central mix concrete batch plant near Heber City in Wasatch County. Cement and fly ash are delivered by trucks, pneumatically loaded by conveyors , and stored in silos. Cement and fly ash are transferred to the central mix ing drum along with aggregate and sand and then mixed with water. Admixtures are also delivered by truc ks and transferred by electric pumps. The batch plant is enclosed and controlled with a dust collector. The facility includes a crusher and screen plant to process 350,000 tons of recycled concrete per year. A diesel -fired engine provides power to the equi pment on site. The facility also operates a boiler. Annual concrete production is limited to 250,000 cubic yards.

SourceName: CMC Southern Post Utah- Steel Post Manufacturing Plant(Site ID: 10024)
GeneralDescription: CMC Southern Post Utah operates a steel post manufacturing plant located in Brigham City, Box Elder County , that specializes in T- or Ranch posts for wire range fences. The post stock material is shipped to the site from a mill , and the sized posts are stacked in a staging area near the anchor plate attachment press. Cut posts are fed into the plate press one by one. The anchor plates have two (2) holes in them , which are placed over the knurls on the bottom of the posts. The press pounds down on the knurls to rivet the plate to the post. Plated posts are then hung on steel hangers on a conveyor line. The conveyor line takes the posts through a preheat ed oven and then through the paint dip tanks. The posts are dried in the main drying oven, and then the process is repeat ed to dip the bottoms of the posts with other coatings. Once dry, the posts are bundled and stockpiled for shipment. The main oven is exhausted to the warming oven. The wire hangers are sent to the burn -off oven every couple of days to remove the built -up pai nt.

GeneralDescription: Canyon Fuel Company , LLC operates a coal mine in Dugout Canyon. Coal from the mine operations is deposited on a covered conveyor belt that feeds the coal to a transfer building and then one of two stackers and pile s according to ash content or other parameters. The coal from the piles is bottom fed onto a belt to the crusher building and then to a belt to the storage bins. The bins are surge bins, which feed to a truck loadout with a drop chute for loading. All coal is removed from site by trucks. Leased trucks transfer a portion of the coal while the rest is sold to other companies and dropped to those company trucks. The crusher building does have water sprays available if needed. However, it is totally enclosed. The mine belt also has water sprays available also. The remainder of the plant is watered as needed by on - site water hoses. The mine produces up to 7 million tons of coal per year.

Project_Description: The UVU#2 tank battery facility was previously equipped with a combustor to control VOC emissions from the crude oil and produced water tanks. Based on a recent source sampling and testing results conducted by the source, Citation has confirmed high percentages of non -combustible gas streams (carbon dioxide an d nitrogen) that prevent the flare from burning the VOCs in the gas streams. SourceName: Citation Oil & Gas Corp.- Pineview Tank Battery and Gas Plant(Site ID: 15513)
GeneralDescription: Citation Oil & Gas Corp. (Citation) operates a crude oil and natural gas tank battery and gas plant at the Pineview Facility loca tion. At the tank battery, crude oil, natural gas, and produced water are processed from an emulsion stream extracted from wells using electric submersible pumps and pumping units. Fluid from the wellheads enters a separator where the oil and water are sep arated from gas. The separated gas is sent to the adjoining gas plant. The liquids from the separators are routed to crude oil and produced water storage tanks at the lower portion of the tank battery. Emissions from the crude oil storage tanks are control led by a flare. The crude oil is sent off site via truck loading , while the produced water is piped for off-site injection within the field. Gas is routed to the gas plant. This facility will process up to 146,000 barrels (1 barrel = 42 gallons) of crude o il per year. Produced water from the separator is stored on -site at the upper portion of the tank battery before being shipped off -site through a flowline for injection. The gas plant processes up to 532.2 million standard cubic feet of natural gas per y ear. The gas plant treats the field gas using glycol and a separator to remove any leftover liquids. The gas is then cooled and compressed for storage in bullet tanks before being piped to the sales point for truck loading.

SourceName: Comfort Research, LLC. - Polystyrene Filled Products Manufacturing(Site ID: 15735)
GeneralDescription: Comfort Research, LLC (Comfort Research) owns/operates a polystyrene -filled product manufacturing facility in Tremonton, Box Elder County. These products include bean bag chairs and other furniture filled with expanded polystyrene (EPS) foam beads. The facility operates three (3) production lines. The first production line inflates EPS beads using an expander unit. The EPS beads are inflat ed by blowing steam across the beads in a steam chamber. The steam is generated using a 6.28 MMBtu/hr boiler. After initial expansion, the beads move to the fluidized bed section of the expander unit for partial drying and are then transferred to the fir st-pass stabilization/holding tent. The beads are allowed to stabilize for several hours in porous tents. After stabilizing, the beads are transferred back to the expander unit for final expansion. The beads are then transferred to a porous holding tent for final curing. The second and third production lines are used for filling bean bag products with EPS beads, sewing , and packaging.

GeneralDescription: EP Energy E&P Company, L.P. has requested a new AO for the Lake Fork Ranch 3-25B4 Crude Oil and Natural Gas Production Tank Battery in Duchesne Cou nty. At the site, an electric pumpjack brings oil to the surface and produced fluids are routed to a hea ter-treater fueled by natural gas. Produced gas is routed offsite via a pipeline and produced water an d oil are stored in separate tanks heated by natura l gas combustion. There are two crude oil storage tanks and one produced water storage tank, each with capacities of 625 barrels. Crude oil and produced water are transported offsite via tanker trucks. Emission points at the site include the heaters and heater-treater, losses from the oil and water tank s, tanker truck loading, and fugitive emissions from v arious components. Emissions from the crude oil storage tanks will be controlled by a combustion de vice. The equipment listed in this AO will be inst alled within 18 months of the issuance of this AO. The application for this project was deemed complet e and the draft permit (ITA) was ready for public comment prior to February 1, 2013; however, due to the administrative process of generating documents and publication dates of newspapers, the public com ment period for this project will begin after Febru ary 1, 2013. OwnerName: DFA Dairy Brands Ice Cream, LLC dba Meadow Gold
SourceName: DFA Dairy Brands Ice Cream, LLC dba Meadow Gold - Ice Cream Manufacturing Facility(Site ID: 12663)
GeneralDescription: DFA Dairy Brands Ice Cream, dba Meadow Gold operates an ice cream plant in St. George, Washington County. Dairy products such as milk and cream are combined in mixers with sweetene rs and flavorings to form a homogenized ice cream product. A steam boiler with a low -NO x burner is used to heat the blending process and the dairy products, as well as heating various parts of the plant. The mixture is flash frozen to a soft -serve ice crea m consistency. At that consistency, the ice cream is mixed with nuts, fruit, fudge, etc. Once mixed the ice cream is pressed into square or round packages depending on the product line. The packaged product is then flash frozen to 20 degrees Fahrenheit and stored, until it is shipped offsite by refrigerated truck trailers. A wet cooling tower is used to evaporate heat from the chillers in the refrigeration process. Wastewater from the plant is collected and treated at the wastewater treatment plant. Gases from the bacterial digesting of the waste in the wastewater treatment plant are collected. These gases are used to operate the boiler that controls the temperature for the wastewater in the storage tank. Bio -solids are collected from the wastewater storag e tank bottom and shipped offsite to a landfill. Treated water is released in the city wastewater stream. A flare is used at the wastewater treatment plant in the event that the gas pressure exceeds the set level, before it can be used in the biogas boiler .

SourceName: GGUSA Hyrum LLC - Renewable Natural Gas Facility(Site ID: 16231)
GeneralDescription: GGUSA Hyrum LLC (GGUSA) has requested to construct a Renewable Natural Gas (RNG) Facility which will treat the biogas (raw gas) from anaerobic digestors and deliver the treated gas to a natural gas pipeline dist ribution system in Hyrum, Cache County. The feed stream for the digestor will be waste from the adjacent Swift Beef Company, Incorporated (Swift Beef) beef processing plant. The raw gas from the anaerobic digestors will be sent to a Vacuum Adsorption Ves sel, which houses iron substrate that is used to control H 2S content. The gas stream is then sent through a molecular gate pressure swing adsorption (PSA) skid to remove CO 2, H 2S, and H 2O in a single step. Methane, N 2, and O 2 pass through the molecular gate as "product gas" and are injected into the pipeline. The removed contaminants (CO 2, H 2S, and H 2O), known as PSA "tail gas" or "waste gas" will either be sent to the flare stack for release to ambient air without combustion or combusted for odor control. The flare will utilize natural gas for a pilot flame and ad ditional natural gas as enrichment gas to ensure combustion.
Project_Description: GGUSA has requested to construct a RNG Facility which will treat the biogas from anaerobic digestors and deliver the treated gas to a natural gas pipeline distribution system in H yrum, Cache County. The feed stream for the anaerobic digestor will be waste from the adjacent Swift Beef processing plant. Under nor mal operations, the raw gas from the digestor is treated with an H 2S VAV and a PSA skid. The H2S VAV will reduce H 2S in the gas stream to no more than 50 ppm, and the PSA skid will filter out the remaining H 2S, as well as CO 2 and H 2O. The H2S, CO 2, and H 2O waste stream is then directed to the flare stack, where it may or may not be combusted. The flare is utilized as a control device for odor control. During non -normal operating scenarios, such as emergency or maintenance operations, the RNG plant will not operate.

SourceName: Geneva Rock Products- Logan Wash Plant & Concrete Batch Plant(Site ID: 12246)
GeneralDescription: Geneva Rock Products produces concrete and washed a ggregate at their plant in Logan, Cache County. Raw aggregate material is hauled into the site from an outside mining location. The material is then loaded into the wash/screening plant. The material is transferred to a wet screen via a conveying syst em. The material is saturated with water once it enters the wet screen. The different size material is sep arated in the screen and deposited into storage piles. The fine material (sand) drops to the bottom level of the screen and is transferred to a screw washer which s eparates and removes some of the water from the san d. The sand is then transferred to a storage pile. The material that is brought into the plant has approx imately 13% silt content. This is retained in the water and is lost in the production process. Finished materi al from the storage piles are sold and removed from the sit e or moved on to the concrete batch plant. The conc rete batch plant receives aggregate and sand from the wa sh plant. Cement and cement supplement are brought to the plant from an outside location. Hot water is supplied to the plant from two (2) natural gas wat er heaters. All ingredients are brought together and m ixed. Bin vents are used to control emissions from the concrete batch plant. Cement trucks haul mixed conc rete off site to locations in the area. Local utili ty power is supplied to the facility, and a diesel gen erator provides supplement power to the facility, operating for 1,000 hrs per 12-month rolling total. The wash plant will process up to 400,000 tons of w ashed aggregate per year. The concrete batch plant will produce up to 150,000 cubic yards of concrete per year. The Logan plant has two (2) natural gas water heaters in the 2009 AO rated at 4 MMBtu/hr, a nd two (2) MMBtu/hr that supply hot water to the concrete batch plant.

SourceName: Grain Craft, LLC. - Ogden Flour Mill(Site ID: 10936)
GeneralDescription: Grain Craft, LLC (Grain Craft) owns/operates a soft and hard wheat elevator storage and flour processing mill in Weber County. The mill limits the processing plant to the use and production of only wheat products. Wheat is received at the mill by truck and rail car. The wheat is unloaded into a reclaiming pit. It is then augured and conveyed to an elevator storage facility. Before the wheat can be made into flour, it is conveyed from the storage to the cleaning house, where it is cleaned by air mixing and agitation. A baghouse is used to collect the waste material from the cleaning process. The waste material collected from th e wheat cleaning process is sold to animal feed processing facilities. From the wheat cleaning house, the wheat is transferred to the mill through conveyor legs. The wheat is processed into flour using a series of rollers that crush the wheat. Sifte rs separate the fine flour product from flour that is too coarse. The coarse product is routed back through the crushing process until all of the product passes through the sifters that are set to the correct size. The flour processed through the mill is used for bread and pastries. The flour is packaged and sold in bags or shipped in bulk to various baking companies and commercial stores throughout the United States. The mill currently operates 28 baghouses for the control of air emissions. All materi al collected by the baghouses is conveyed back into the mill for additional processing into flour or is sold to animal food processes.

SourceName: Granite Construction Company- Magna Asphalt Plant(Site ID: 16244)
GeneralDescription: Granite Construction Company (Granite) operates an aggregate processing and hot mix asphalt (HMA) plant in Magna, Salt Lake County. To produce H MA, raw materials such as virgin aggregates, recycled asphalt pavement (RAP), and asphalt cement are hauled on site and stored. Virgin aggregates are stored in one large stockpile segregated by concrete partitions based on size. The RAP is sized and sorted via crushers, conveyors , and screens and then stockpiled. Asphalt cement is stored onsite in asphalt cement storage tanks. Both virgin aggregates and processed RAP are load ed into hoppers using a front -end loader and conveyed into the asphalt plant drum v ia a conveyor system. Asphalt cement is added to the aggregate/RAP mix within the drum , and the final asphalt produced is conveyed to one of the storage silos via a drag slat conveyor. Asphalt is then loaded into haul trucks for offsite transport. Annu al production is limited to 650,000 tons each of HMA and processed aggregate material (including RAP).

Project_Description: Under Condition II.B.5.a of AO DAQE -AN162440001 -25, dated August 29, 2025, Granite is permitted to produce up to 650,000 tons of processed aggregate material annually. The DAQ is adjusting this condition to clarify that "processed aggregate material" includes reclaimed asphalt paveme nt (RAP). Therefore, the condition will be updated to the following: "The owner/operator shall not produce more than 650,000 tons combined of processed aggregate material (including RAP) per rolling 12 -month period." RAP production was incorporated int o the site -wide potential -to-emit (PTE) estimates and dispersion modeling analyses in the Notice of Intent (NOI) for AO DAQE -AN162440001 -25. No emissions are increasing. There are no changes to equipment or operations taking place at the Magna Asphalt Plan t. This is an administrative amendment to provide clarity to ensure the combined RAP and virgin aggregate annual production does not exceed 650,000 tons. No public comment period is required for this project.

SourceName: ICU Medical, Inc.- Salt Lake Medical Products Manufacturing(Site ID: 11644)
GeneralDescription: ICU Medical, Inc. (ICU) manufactures critical care medical products. Solvent bonding, cleaning, injection molding, and final assembly are used in the manufacturing processes. As parts are assembled, small amounts of solvent are used to bond pieces together. The solvent is stored and dispensed in small , vapor -tight containers that dispense small droplets of solvent onto th e items to be bonded. Small amounts of VOC -containing solvent are used to clean various manufactured plastic products and equipment. Generally, solvents are applied to rags and wiped on the products or equipment. Raw plastic products are formed by injectio n molding. Plastic granules are stored in storage silos until they are pneumatically piped to an injection mold machine storage device. The raw products include polyvinyl chloride, polycarbonate, polypropylene , and other plastics. The VOC content of the pl astic pellets is very small. The plastic pellets are heated in the injection -molding machine and extruded into molds. Once the parts are set and stable, they are pulled from the machine and allowed to cool before being transferred to another part of the fa cility or packaged. Many of the parts are assembled into ready -to-use systems and shipped to medical supply vendors for sale and further dispensing to hospitals.

SourceName: Kennecott Utah Copper LLC- Mine & Copperton Concentrator(Site ID: 10571)
GeneralDescription: Rio Tinto Kennecott Utah Copper LLC (Kennecott) operates the Copperton Concentrator that receives the ore from the Bingham Canyon Mine , which is run through the grinding lines and made into a slurry. The slurry from the ore is fed into the flotation circuits , which are mixed with reagents and aerated to float the copper from the ore and routed to the refinery to be further processed.
Project_Description: Kennecott is proposing to use some of the tailings generated at the Concentrator as feed material for the Paste Plant at the Bingham Canyon Mine. Kennecott requires the use of an emergency generator (250 -kW standby emergency generator, (EPA) Tier 4 certified engine) to have backup power at the Concentrator in the event of a power fail ure. During a power failure , the materials being directed at the Paste Plant could slide back through the pipe to the Concentrator where the force of the material would present a dangerous scenario to equipment and people in the area. The emergency generat or would ensure that pumping capacity remains in place in the event of a power outage to mitigate the potential hazards. Kennecott is also proposing to add a mobile diesel -powered air compressor unit (540 -HP diesel engine, (EPA ) Tier 4 certified engine) to meet compressed air needs at the Concentrator. Both engines require hour limitations that have been included in the permit modification. Process Description Ore from the Bingham Canyon Mine is stockpiled in the A -frame enclosure. The ore is fed from the enclosure to the grinding lines through the semi -autogenous grinding (SAG) mills and ball mill , making the ore into slurry. The slurry is se nt through cyclone clusters , the cyclone overflow is fed into the flotation circuits, and the cyclone underflow is sent to the ball mills for further size reduction before being sent back to the cyclones and on to the flotation circuit. In the flotation c ircuits, overflow is mixed with reagents and aerated to float copper and other valuable by-products from the ore. Concentrate is pumped to the existing regrinding circuit, while tailings gravity flows to a collection trench. Tailings from the collection tr enches are fed to the tailings thickener feed distributor and then transported by gravity flow to the tailings impoundment for final deposition. Thickener overflow is pumped back to the process water reservoir.

SourceName: Kennecott Utah Copper, LLC-Smelter & Refinery(Site ID: 10346)
GeneralDescription: Rio Tinto Kennecott Utah Copper LLC (KUC) operates a copper smelter and refinery in Salt Lake County, Utah. The smelter employs flash smelting technology with flash conversion technology to produce copper anodes and high -concentration sulfur dioxide gases. The gases are treated by electrostatic precipitators (ESP), baghouses, scrubbers, and a high -efficiency double -contact acid plant. The refinery uses an electrolytic refining process to convert the Smelter -produced anode copper to cathode copper and also recovers precious metals from the electrolytic refining slimes in a precious metals circuit. The copper ore conc entrates received at the smelter are first dewatered and then dried to reduce the moisture content. The dried concentrate is then blended with fluxes and secondary copper -bearing materials. This mixture is fed to a flash smelting furnace where the ore is m elted and reacts to produce copper matte, a molten solution of copper sulfide mixed with iron sulfide. The Outokumpu flash smelting process used at the smelter is a closed process that captures the SO 2-rich off -gases from the furnace for the production of sulfuric acid. The copper matte from the smelting furnace is next converted to blister copper (approximately 98% pure copper) by oxidization to remove the remaining sulfur as SO 2 gas and the iron as a ferrous oxide slag. The smelter uses a continuous coppe r conversion process in which solid matte granules are fed to a flash smelting furnace -like vessel. The molten slag from converting is cooled, processed in slag concentrators to remove residual copper, and ultimately disposed of in on -site waste piles. The SO 2 gases from smelting and converting are vented to a sulfuric acid plant. Molten blister copper is transferred from the converting vessel to an anode furnace for fire refining to further remove residual impurities and oxygen. The blister copper is reduc ed in the anode furnace to remove oxygen by injecting natural gas, producing a high -purity copper. The molten copper from the anode furnace is poured in molds to cast solid copper ingots called anodes. DAQE -AN103460063 -24 Page 4 The anode copper produced at the Smelter is moved t o the copper Refinery co -located near the Smelter, where it is further purified using an electrolytic process to obtain the high -purity cathode copper sold.

GeneralDescription: Kimberly -Clark Corporation (KCC) operates a diaper manufacturing plant in Ogden, Weber County. The KCC Ogden plant is a disposable garment manufacturing facility. Absorbent fluff (made from cellulose and polymer), a protective liner, and an outer plastic shell ent er an automated assembly line to form various garments. The fluff material enters the form machine at the front of the line and is uniformly shredded and mixed. The combined fluff moves by conveyor through a compression stage to form the absorbent bulk of the garment. Once the fluff has been shaped, the protective inside liner and plastic outer shell are added. When all three components have been combined , the final shape is cut and the garment is sealed. The production lines are all vented to cyclones and/or baghouses to control particulate emissions and recycle material. This facility has a recla im system where rejected garments and fluff are separated and either baled into fluff bricks, fluff bales, or tailings bales. All processed waste material is sold .

Project_Description: Kinder Morgan has requested to update the Bluebell Compressor Station (CS) as follows: -Installation of a flare (FL -1) to control emissions from existing condensate tanks CS T -211, CS T -212, CS T -213, and CS T -214 -Replacement of existing combustor (COM -2) with a flare (FL -2) to control emission s from existing condensate tanks GPP TK -870 and GPP -TK-874 -Update represented potential to emit from condensate truck loading activities Process Description The Bluebell Gas Plant's functions to dehydrate field gas and extract NGLs. Field gas enters the facility through trunk lines. The inlet gas stream is directed to the slug catcher , where free water and condensate are separated from the field gas. The water and condensate are directed to the existing storage tanks before being trucked from the facility . Field gas from the slug catcher is directed to the inlet compressors. Any liquid collected from the second and third stage inlet compressor scrubbers is directed to an intermediate 3 -phase flash separator (V-405). The condensate from V -405 is routed to tanks GPP -870 and GPP -874, which are controlled with a combustor. After compression Ethylene Glycol (EG) is injected into the gas to prevent hydrates from forming prior to the gas going through a series of heat exchangers and a propane chiller to cool dow n the gas. EG and NGL are separated from the gas in the cold separator. The rich EG is sent to a flash tank , where free gas is separated and sent to a combustor for control. The EG is then sent to a regenerator. Water vapor from the EG regenerator is sent to TK -837, where the uncondensed vapor is controlled by a combustor. NGLs are then sent to a de -ethanizer tower , where light end hydrocarbons are removed and sent to the fuel gas system. The NGL is then sent to pressurized bullet tanks for storage prior to being trucked off site. The Bluebell Gas Plant and the Bluebell CS is equipped with a flare to manage unplanned pressure relief events, planned and unplanned maintenance activities, and plant emergency shutdowns.

Compressed gas is then directed to a Sulfa check, Ultrafab or similar scrubber for removal of residual H2S. Treate d gas is discharged from the scrubber to a TEG dehydration unit, equipped with a flash tank and still vent condenser to remove water. The flash tank gas is redirected to the compression inlet for recovery to the gas stream. Compressed, dry gas is discharge d from the site by pipeline and delivered to the Altamont Main Gas Plant. Incoming field gas is received in the inlet slug catcher and subsequently routed to an inlet separator. Liquids removed from the gas in the inlet slug catcher will be directed to th e pit tank. Water and condensate separate in the pit tank. Water is loaded out by tanker trucks and condensate is directed to three (3) condensate tanks from which it is loaded out by tanker trucks. Vapors from the pit tank and three (3) condensate tanks a re directed to a combustor. Alternatively, liquids from the inlet separator may be directed to the vapor recovery system that consists of a vapor recovery tower (VRT) and vapor recovery unit (VRU). The liquids exiting the VRT are routed to the 400 bbl pit tank.

GeneralDescription: Liberty Safe and Security Products, Inc . (Liberty Safe) owns and operates a safe and gun vault manufacturing plant in Payson, Utah County. The safes are cut, formed, and folded from rolled coil steel, then welded together in the metal shop. After forming and welding, the safes are placed on a metal prep line for grinding and sanding. The safes are then conveyed through a 5 -stage washer to be cleaned. After exiting the washer, the safes are oven dried before entering into either one of two powder coating booths or one of two wet coating booths for surface coating. Some safes are painted with a primer, baked, sanded, top coated with liquid paint , and baked , while others are painted directly with a powder coating. Each powder booth is vented to a cartridge filtering system that recirculates filt ered exhaust air back into the building. There are no VOCs present in the powder coating process. Powder -coated safes are conveyed to a curing oven and transferred into the final assembly area to be finished. The safes that have been painted with a prime r are conveyed to a sanding booth. After sanding, they receive a final coat of automotive -quality wet paint. They are then hand -sprayed with a coat of glossy paint in one of several colors. The wet painted safes are conveyed through a drying oven and allow ed to air-dry. Paint overspray and VOC emissions are pulled through each booth's bank of paint particulate filters. All of the paint booths are equipped with paint arrestor filters. The VOCs pass through the filters and are vented through each booth's exha ust stack. DAQE -AN125380010 -24 Page 4

Project_Description: Liberty Safe is proposing to install a sheetrock shredding operation to convert used sheetrock slabs to crushed gypsum powder. Liberty Safe uses sheetrock as a fire retardant in their safe manufacturing process . With the current system, the sheetrock is shaped with a wet -jet cutting system , and the excess sheetrock is hauled away via a third party. The installation of the sheetrock shredder allows Liberty Safe to process the waste material into a crushed powder for disposal. The crushed powder gypsum is hauled off by a third party . The new sheetrock shredding operation is composed of a large hopper that sits above a grinder, a product recovery cyclone, an airlock, a screw auger, and a gypsum collector unit. Waste sheetrock is fed into the large hopper where it then falls into the grinder to be mechanically crushed into a powdered gypsum material. The material falls into an air stream , which pneumatically conveys the material into a product recovery cyclone. The material collected by the cyclone passes through an airlock into the s crew conveyor and is deposited into a haul -off dumpster. The cyclone is not considered a control technology because the cyclone is integral to the sheetrock shredding process; thus, the cyclone is considered as being a part of the process. The air that is separated from the gypsum powder in the product recovery cyclone is sent through a filter baghouse that achieves a 95% reduction of particulate matter emissions.

Project_Description: Linde has requested to update the flaring operations at the SLC plant. Linde has reviewed flare operations and determine d a large margin of safet y in the annual flaring events, the duration of the events, and the quality of fuel being combusted. The updated flare operations result in an overall decrease in the PTE emissions of NO x, CO, SO x, PM 10, PM 2.5, and VOCs. In the flare operations analysis, it was  determined that the shutdown operations of the flare were not included in the previous permit. The addition of the flare shutdown operation result ed in a CO 2e increase. The addition of the requirements on the flare ha s been added for this permit modification. The revised PTE emissions for the SLC plant lower the site -wide PTEs below the major source classification for sources located in the ozone nonattainment designation in the Northern Wasatch Front nonattainment area. Process Description Linde's SLC Plant consists of a single Steam Methane Reformation (SMR) and associated equipment to provide up to 26 MMscfd of 99.9% pure hydrogen. The plant employs steam methane reforming to convert feedstock gas (refinery gases and/or natural gas) plus steam to pure hydrogen. The emitting units from the plant are: Combustion gas from the SMR furnace stack; Combustion gas from a small natural gas purge pilot flame associated with a flare; Steam vent from the boil er feed water de -aerator unit; and Fugitive equipment component s leak. In a closed system inside the SMR, desulfurized feed gas is mixed with process steam , which is then fed to catalyst tubes located in a down -fired, downflow reformer furnace. The catal yst tubes are filled with nickel catalysts. The reaction to the catalyst is at equilibrium , which is limited based on furnace outlet temperature and pressure. The reaction does not produce air emissions and is endothermic. The reaction does require addi tional heat, supplied by downward -firing ultra-low NO x design burners located at the top of the furnace. The burners provide indirect heat for the process. The burner exhaust does not directly contact the reaction gases. These burners are the only sourc e of SMR air emissions. The rest of the process equipment performs two (2) major functions. A series of heat exchangers, boilers, and a steam drum recover SMR furnace heat to generate process steam and steam for export. At the end of the process, a reco very unit called a PSA uses adsorbent beds and cyclic changes in pressure to separate SMR reaction products and provide pure export hydrogen from the top of the beds and waste gas from the bottom of the beds. This waste gas, also referred to as PSA off -gas or tail -gas, is circulated back to the SMR burners and provides most of the plant fuel requirements. Additional fuel for the SMR furnace comes from local natural gas. Refinery fuel gas from a refinery can be used as a backup if the H 2S content of that fuel is low enough to allow proper burner operation. Since the PSA off -gas is consumed in the furnace, the entire process generates few waste streams. During emergency maintenance, process overpressure events , and startup/showdown, the process gases are routed to a flare and destroyed .

SourceName: Lisbon Valley Mining Company LLC- Lisbon Valley Open Pit Copper Mine(Site ID: 11462)
GeneralDescription: Lisbon Valley Mining Co. (LVMC) is in San Juan County, about 18 miles southeast of La Sal, Utah, and about 5 miles west of the Colorado border. LVMC is a surface copper mine with ore crushing, a heap leaching site, and a solvent extraction electrowinning (SX -EW) processing plant. The ore is blasted from the surrounding site, it is loaded into trucks and then hauled to a heap leaching site. The leaching site uses sulfuric acid to extract the copper from the rock. When the ore has yielded all the copper, the soil is taken back by truck and the site is ultimately reclaimed. The copper goes through the SX -SW process after the soil is removed. The sulfuric acid is reclaimed and the copper is collected.

SourceName: Malnove Incorporated of Utah- Packaging Manufacturing Operation(Site ID: 14858)
GeneralDescription: Malnove Incorporated of Utah (Malnove) operates a packaging manufacturing and packaging solutions facility in Clearfield, Davis County. To begin the printing process, Malnove produces a plate for each primary color and any specialty colors required for a pr oject. Equipment used to produce these plates includes a plate imager, a plate processor, and a proofing device. The printed substrate is cut into sheets using a sheeter. The cut substrate is then fed into printing press lines where it is dampened, inked, and dried. As part of the drying process, spray powder is applied to the sheets. The printed sheets are cut using a die -cutting machine and glued into boxes. Scrap substrate is deposited into a bailer. Small inkjet printers are used to add additional produ ct information to the box. Cleaning solutions are used to remove excess printing inks, oils, and residual paper from the press equipment. The cleaning may be done manually, e.g., shop towel, or with an automatic blanket wash system. Evaporative VOC and HAP emissions come from the inks, wetting solutions, glues, and cleaning solutions. Particulate emissions are generated by the spray powder used in the drying process and the bailer. These particulate -emitting processes are controlled using a dust collection system.

The plants subject to this AO shall be temporarily operated for a period of not more than 180 working days at any location. Prior to the commencement of operation at a location, the owner/operator shall submit a Notice of Temporary Relocation to the Director and obtain a Temporary Relocation Approval Letter. The emission control measures required in the conditions of this AO shall apply to all of the locations at which the source approved by this AO operates. This AO limits the source to emissions that are below the major source threshold, m aking it a "Synthetic Minor" source. NSPS, 40 CFR Part 60, Subparts A, I, OOO, and IIII, and MACT, 40 CFR 63, Subparts A and ZZZZ regulations apply to this portable source. This source is considered a Title V area source. SourceName: Merit Medical Systems, Inc.- Medical Device Manufacturing Plant(Site ID: 15967)
GeneralDescription: Merit Medical Systems, Inc. (Merit Medical), located in South Jordan, manufactures disposable medical items in the cardiology, radiology, oncology, endoscopy, and critical care fields. The process i ncludes natural gas -fired boilers on the manufacturing campus of five (5) buildings. The three (3) boilers rated at 5 MMBtu/hr are located at the Bean building. The other boilers rated at less than 5 MMBtu/hr each are located throughout the campus of build ings. Four (4) diesel -fired emergency generators, operating 100 hrs/yr, are located throughout the campus. Emissions from the manufacture of silicon wafers in Building 87 include acid gases that are controlled with a scrubber. The campus emits VOCs from it s work, including metal parts cleaning, plastic resin molding, plastic coating, sensor fabrication/soldering, device assembly/adhesive bonding, cleaning, injection molding, and printing. In the metal parts cleaning processes, organic material is cleaned of f the metal parts at a maximum rate of 10 lbs/hr. A pyrolysis device will combust the emissions from the cleaning process.

SourceName: Mountain View Mushrooms- Mushroom Farms(Site ID: 10312)
GeneralDescription: Mountain View Mushrooms (MVM) is a producer and wholesaler of fresh mushrooms located in Fillmore, Millard County. M VM starts the process outdoors by creating compost for the spores to grow on. When the compost is ready, it is added to growing trays and taken inside to a pasteurization room to eliminate undesirable bacteria. Once the trays are prepared, the mushroom spawn is mixed with the compost , and the growing tray is placed in another room for several weeks to allow the spawn to grow. After spawning, the growing trays are placed in a growing room , and mushrooms are harvested several weeks later. MVM harvests mushrooms every day to ship them to a variety of places in the Intermountain West region.

SourceName: MountainWest Pipeline, LLC - Cash Compressor Station(Site ID: 10689)
GeneralDescription: Dominion Energy Questar Pipeline, LLC operates the R.D. Cash Compressor Station located in Summit County on private land along Main Lines 1, 13 and 1 5. The station provides transport of energy resour ces through pipelines, and treats natural gas with merc aptan. No further treatment of energy resources occ urs on site.
Source_Classification: Located in Attainment Area Summit County Airs Source Size: B
Applicable_Federal_Standards: MACT (Part 63), ZZZZ: NESHAP for Stationary Recipro cating Internal Combustion Engines
Project_Description: Dominion Energy requested a modification to their e xisting AO to update the equipment list, re-evaluat e federal applicability, and update the site-name. Ba sed on this request, the following changes were mad e: Two (2) tanks listed in AO DAQE-AN0106890005-11 und er condition II.A.6 were replaced with various new tanks storing condensate, oil, waste water, met hanol, and mercaptan. Mercaptan is a chemical used to  sweeten natural gas before sale. The tanks are list ed for informational purposes only, as mercaptan is neither a VOC nor a HAP. A flare that was previously on site, but not added to equipment list, was added to the approval order as an emission source. It is used to burn off excess merc aptan odors to reduce the impact of the odors on th e local community, which is why it is considered a so urce of emissions and not a control device. Conditions II.A.2-II.A.4 have been updated to ident ify the make, model, and more accurately describe unit's capacity per the direct request of the sourc e. The equipment list was also updated to clarify t he fuel type used for these engines. Conditions II.B.1.c&d have been modified to reflect updated requirements for emergency engines and the re-evaluated calculations. These now reflect curren t requirement conventions for emergency engines. The heater currently in the equipment list is natur al gas fired and has a heat input under 5 MMBtu/hr, and as such is exempt under R307-401-10(1). The equipme nt list has been updated to allow flexibility in replacing the heater. Facility emissions calculations have been updated t o reflect added emissions from the flare and new storage tanks, and recalculated emissions using emi ssion factors from AP 42 3.2 instead of 3.1. Federal rule applicability for the compressor engin e listed in II.A.2 has been updated, as NSPS subpar t JJJJ was erroneously listed as applicable in the pr evious modification. The equipment list now reflect s applicable federal standards.

OwnerName: Nestle USA Prepared Foods Division Inc.
SourceName: Nestle USA Prepared Foods Division Inc.- Prepared Foods Processing Plant(Site ID: 10812)
GeneralDescription: Nestle USA Prepared Food Division Inc. (Nestle) is a frozen food producer located in Utah County. Nestle receives food ingredients and pre -manufactured packaging materials by truck. Food is processed in batches. After each batch, the equipment used is washed, sanitized, and set up for the next batch. The process for each batch is the same. Ingredients are loaded by weight into large carts and pushed to the appropriate food processing line. Depending on the reci pe, the ingredients are then sliced, diced, chopped, mixed, cooked/baked, and/or blanched. The processed food is then packaged on an automated packaging line. Immediately following packaging, the food is conveyed through a large ammonia freezer. The pac kaged food is frozen for four (4) hours. The frozen food then exits the freezer and is placed in cold storage for shipment. Baking is done in four (4) natural gas -fired 5.0 MMBtu/hr baking ovens. The facility uses steam kettles to cook various food. St eam for the steam kettles is produced by two (2) natural gas -fired 89.61 MMBtu/hr steam boilers. The ovens and boilers can also be fired on propane in an emergency. Two (2) baghouses control exhaust air from the Semolina flour receiving storage silos. One (1) baghouse controls exhaust air from the dry ingredients room. Several diesel -fired and natural gas -fired emergency generator engines provide power in the event of a power outage.

SourceName: North Davis Cabinet Incorporated- Cabinet Manufacturing Facility(Site ID: 10187)
GeneralDescription: North Davis Cabinet Incorporated (NDC) operates a cabinet manufacturing plant in Clearfield. The plant manufactures and installs residential cabinetry. Production involves processing hardwood lumber and composite wood sheet products with plan ners, saws, shapers , etc. to create individual doors, face frame s, and cabinet parts. These parts are assembled, sanded with wide belt sanders, and final s anded with handheld orbital sanders. Saw dust emissions from the planners, saws, shapers , and wide belt sanders are collected by a baghouse -style dust collector. Emissions from hand sanding operations are collected by a down draft table , which filters the air directly back into the building. The doors, face frames , and cabinet parts are finished with sealers, stains, glazes, and lacquers. Applications of these products vary from wiping, brushing, air spray, and air -assisted airless spray technology by hand and with a spray machine. Emissions from the finished applications are collected by three spray booths and one spray machine with a stack. The painted parts are air dried and assembled to produce individual cabinets. The baghouse is vented inside during th e cold season and usually only vents outside 3 -5 months per year.

SourceName: Nucor Steel(Site ID: 10008)
GeneralDescription: Nucor Steel (Nucor) operates a minimill in Box Elder County. Shredded metal scrap is melted to create new steel products. Steel scrap is charged in the electric arc furnace (EAF). The electric charge heats the scrap until molten. Lime and dolomite are added to the molten steel to react with and float impurities. These impurities are removed as slag. The refined molten steel is then mixed with alloy elements to create the desired composition. The steel mixture is run through the caster to produce the desired mold. The molded slabs or billets are cut to size with gas torches after leaving the caster. The slabs are then sent to the rolling mill. Slabs are heated in the rolling mill to ensure the steel is malleable enough to be reduced to the desired thickness. The reheat furnaces are used to reheat cooled billets before further processing. Nucor is limited to 1.42 million tons of steel production, 8,220 hours per year of melt shop operations, 2.34 billion scf of natural gas, and 2.8 million gallons per year of propane.

OCIS submitted an NOI dated February 29, 2024 , reque sting to increase the total glass pull throughput and binder solids limit (to account for the increase in glass pull throughput) at the Nephi Plant. The increase in total glass throughput (from 38,325 tpy to 43,800 tpy) will not increase VOCs emissions. (PCC Structurals) operates an aluminum and stainless steel investment casting plant in Ogden, Weber County. PCC Structurals uses the lost wax process to create special order metal casting parts for customers. Wax is first melted, tempered, and injected in to molds. The wax cools in the molds, creating a hardened wax pattern. Once the wax pattern has been constructed, it is dipped in a water -based slurry, coated with ceramic sand, and dried. This process is repeated until the ceramic shell reaches the desire d thickness. Wax is then melted from the ceramic shell in an autoclave, leaving a hollow ceramic shell. The shell is heated in a mold burnout furnace to cure. Once the mold is cured, it is filled with molten metal. In the aluminum process, molten metal is kept in large covered pots (melt units), ladled from the melt units, and poured into the ceramic mold. The mold is then quenched in either an oil or water/polymer quench. In the stainless steel process, molten metal is kept in crucibles (melt furnaces) th at are covered with a blanket of argon gas. Metal is transferred into a ladle and poured into the ceramic mold; the ceramic mold is then covered with metal cans to remove oxygen from the atmosphere. In this process, to break the ceramic shell from the meta l, the bulk of the shell is removed using grinders and hammers in a small booth. In both divisions, excess metal and residual ceramic shell is removed using saws, abrasive cutting tools, and blasting equipment. A heated sodium hydroxide bath then removes ceramic cores and the ceramic shell that remains deep within the casting. Castings then undergo various finishing operations and shaping to meet final dimensions. In the stainless steel process, some castings are immersed into nitric -based acidic solutions for electropolishing or acid dipping processes. PCC Structurals then does final inspections for the castings using non -destructive testing processes, inspections, and measurements. DAQE -AN109390008 -21 Page 4

SourceName: R & S Marble Inc.- Cultured Marble Manufacturing Plant(Site ID: 10017)
GeneralDescription: R & S Marble Inc. is the owner of a Cultured Marble Facility in Brigham City, Box Elder County. This facility produces and installs cultured marble for kitchen and bathroom surfaces , including showers, countertops , and bathtubs. The manufacturing process begins with mold pr eparation. Using a pressure pot and spray gun (in the spray booth), a mold is prepared by applying 15 -20 mils of gel coat onto a mold. Polyester resin is then measured by weight and placed in the mixing bowl of an upright column mixer. The resin is catalyz ed and mixed for a predetermined time based on the total batch size. The appropriate size and type of fillers are added to the batch and mixed thoroughly to achieve proper wetting of the filler and consistency of the matrix. One (1) or more color pigments are added to the matrix to achieve a veined pattern. The material is then drawn from the bowl and cast on the previously gel -coated mold to achieve the desired effects. When the casting process is completed and the product has cured, the finished product is released from the mold. The final step is to grind off -mold flash.

SourceName: Ramsey Hill Exploration, LLC- RHEx Vernal Sand Mine(Site ID: 15971)
PhysicalStreet: 8.5 Miles North on US-191 from Vernal Main Street
GeneralDescription: Ramsey Hill Exploration, LLC (RHEx) operates a sand processing plant at the Vernal Sand Mine. RHEx uses explosives to loosen aggregate material for mining. A track hoe/excavator loads the mined material into trucks. The trucks deposit the mined material into storage piles. The material in the storage piles is fed into a crusher for processing. The crushed material is then fed into a wet processing plant, which includes a wet screen. The wet screen separates the desired sand product from the reject material. Both wet sand product and reject material is stored in storage piles. Trucks transport the reject material to a reclamation area on site. The wet product storage pile feeds the rotary sand dryers. The sand dryers use propane as fuel and remove moisture from the product. The dry sand is stored in storage bin/silos, which are controlled by a fabric filter or bin vent. Trucks ship material from the storage bins/silos to customers off-site. The facility produces up to 3,066,000 tons of processed sand per year.

GeneralDescription: Rockwell, LLC (Rockwell) is requesting a new AO for an existing composite window well manufacturing facility in Springville, Utah County. Rockwell uses a manufacturing process that uses polyester resin to create window wells for the construction market. The manufacturing process includes the use of polyester resin molds, curing ovens, and sanding and finishing operations.
Source_Classification: Located in Southern Wasatch Front O3 NAA, Provo UT PM 2.5 NAA Utah County Airs Source Size: B
Applicable_Federal_Standards: None
Project_Description: Rockwell was previously o perating under UAC R307 -401-9, Small Source Exemption, and is expanding operations. Rockwell uses a manufacturing process that uses polyester resin to create window wells for the construction market. The manufacturing process includes the use of polyester resin molds, curing ovens, and sanding and finishing operations.  Raw materials (parts) are measured and loaded into a mold. The mold is closed , and all emissions are evacuated via a vacuum pump. The part in the mold is then transferred to a curing oven. O nce cured, the part is removed from the mold to be sanded and mounted. As part of the finishing process, an automotive clear coat is sprayed over the window well. Emissions from the process primarily come from polyester resin in the production of the w indow well and from polyurethane clear coat and adhesive. The emissions from the resin come through vacuum pumps and are removed from the building by turbines. The emissions from the clear coat and adhesive come through a spray gun and are removed from the building by turbines. Both are filtered.

GeneralDescription: Running Foxes Petroleum, Inc . (Running Foxes) has requested to operate a new data center located in Grand County. Other sources of emissions inc lude fugitive emissions, truck loading, pneumatic devices, and a water storage tank.

Applicable_Federal_Standards: None
Project_Description: XCL requested a new A pproval Order to construct and operate a flowback and produced water holding pit. Pit 11 will have a holding capacity of 918,937 barrels (bbl) and be located approximately 3.3 mi les  northwest of Ioka, in Duchesne County, Utah. Flowback and produced water will flow into the pit from the surrounding oilfield. All fluids received will have been processed through an oil -gas separator off -site. The water from the pit will be recycled and used in future fracking operations or disposed of in a nearby injection well. Materials will be transferred via electric pumps. The PTE was estimated using samples from produced water at a similar location. Due to the variability of the materials ente ring the pit, the source will demonstrate compliance through monthly sampling. Two samples will be taken, one from the inlet location and one from the outlet. This will provide any notable differences as a result of heavier materials sinking and or mixing as a result of the continuous flow. These samples will be averaged and analyzed using EPA methods. The concentration results will then be multiplied by the evaporated volume to determine the monthly emissions total. The evaporated volume will be determined using a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system. The material in minus the material out as logged from the inlet and outlet pipes in the system will determine the evaporated volume. One hundred percent of the sample concentrations will be assumed to have evaporated. UDAQ considers this a conservative calculation as differing pollutants may have a lower vapor pressure and therefore be retained in the fluid. Therefore, the heavier materials may evaporate at a rate lower than 100%.

GeneralDescription: Silver Eagle Refining - Woods Cross Inc. (Silver Ea gle) has submitted notification of two projects at its existing facility in Woods Cross. As both projects are exempt under the provisions of R307-401-12, Reduction in Air Pollutants, they have been combine d under this single permitting action for convenien ce. The first project, submitted May 26, 2016, addresse s several changes at existing storage tanks located at the facility. Tank 15 has been retrofitted to stor e yellow wax crude feedstocks. This retrofit consi sts of a new internal floating roof and related internal roo f support structures. The tank is already subject to NSPS Subpart Kb. Also included in this project was a request to update the listed stored content for several tanks. Silver Eagle estimated the potentia l VOC emissions from both the prior and new content configurations and showed an emission reduction of 0.4 tons/year. The second project, submitted July 7, 2016, covers the use of a new instrument-based monitoring methodology. This will more accurately measure cru de oil charge rate, by switching to a flow measurement basis, rather than relying on tank leve l gauges. Also included was the installation of a new diesel additive tank (71 barrels in capacity) which had been left out of the previous tank update (May 26, 2016 submittal). This additional tank is so small in total potential emissions, that it has no impact on the status of the exemption for the May 26, 2016 projec t. This was verified by supplemental information submitted by Silver Eagle on October 3, 2016 which indicated total VOC emissions of less than 0.002 tons/year and total HAP emissions of less than 0.02 lbs/year from this tank. Silver Eagle satisfied the requirements of R307-401 -12, as: 1) neither project increases the potential to emit of any air pollutant or causes emissions of an y new air pollutant, and 2) notified the Director w ithin 60 days of the change having taken place. Thus, bo th projects qualify as exempt under R307-401-12. This update to Silver Eagle's AO serves as acknowle dgement of that fact. Finally, Silver Eagle has requested a reduction in the NO x limitation listed in Condition II.B.2.c and a correction to the emission factors listed in Condit ion II.B.2.d. The limitation in II.B.2.c has been based on outdated AP-42 emission factors carried over fro m an earlier AO and erroneously maintained in II.B.2.d. By correcting to the updated emission fa ctors in the current version of AP-42, the NO x limitation will be reduced to 67.5 tons/year. The Silver Eagle Refinery is located in Davis Count y, a nonattainment area of the NAAQS for PM 2.5 , and a maintenance area for ozone. There is no change i n emissions resulting from the upgraded monitoring methodology, a decrease in potential VOC emissions (0.4 TPY) from the storage tank changes, and a correction to the potential NO x emissions (now set at 67.5 TPY) from the use of up dated emission factors. The resulting PTE totals, in TPY, are as follows: P M 10 (which includes PM 2.5 ) = 14.0, PM 2.5 = 8.48, NO x = 67.5, SO x = 2.03, CO = 85.04, VOC = 65.42, total HAPs = 11.8 1 and CO 2e = 98,000. This air quality AO authorizes the project with the following conditions and failure to comply with an y of the conditions may constitute a violation of this o rder. GeneralDescription: On October 31, 2017 Chevron Products Company (Chevr on) submitted a NOI for a Tier 3 Fuels Project. The project involves changes to the existing Crude Unit to allow production of low-sulfur gasoline to meet federal Tier 3 standards. Two (2) new reactor s will be added in the Crude Unit between the napht ha stabilizer column and the naphtha splitter column. These reactors convert sulfur containing compounds to promote removal with existing downstream equipment. The new reactors are not themselves new air emission units, nor do any new emission units need to be installed as part of this project. Separately, Chevron is also installing a new diesel -fired emergency generator, with a rating of 400 kW , at the existing hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) unit. The installation of this generator is unrelated to the Tier 3 project, but for permitting purposes is being inclu ded with this action merely as a matter of timing a nd convenience. Chevron's Salt Lake Refinery is located in Davis Co unty; which is a maintenance area for ozone, and a NAA of the NAAQS for PM 2.5 . Chevron is a listed source in Sections IX.H.2 an d IX.H.12 of the SIP. The Salt Lake Refinery is subject to multiple feder al (NSPS and NESHAP) subparts, but none are directl y affected by the Tier 3 Fuels project. The new gene rator is subject to the provisions of 40 CFR 60 Sub part IIII. A calculation of the potential emission increases w as performed for all units which might be affected by the project. This included the effects of increase d utilization, increased sulfur loading to the SRU, and additional equipment leaks. The increase in emissi ons is estimated at 0.4 tons/year of SO 2 and 2.1 tons/year of VOC. These increases are in actual em issions and have no effect on potential emissions. The emissions from the new emergency generator are calculated at the following ton/year amounts: PM 10 = 0.02, PM 2.5 (a subset of PM 10 ) = 0.02, NO x = 1.6, SO 2 = 0.05, CO = 0.17, and VOC = 0.05; however, Chevron will include the emissions from this genera tor under the existing plant-wide caps. Therefore, Chevron's current SIP Cap values and annual PTEs wi ll remain in place at the following ton/year amounts: PM 10 = 261.0, PM 2.5 = 110.0, NO x = 766.5, SO 2 = 383.3, CO = 991.0, and VOC = 1,242.0. Total GHG emissions are estimated at 988,625 tons/y r as CO 2e. GeneralDescription: On February 2, 2016, Chevron Products Co – SL Refin ery (Chevron) submitted an NOI for a proposed project entitled the "Boiler #7 Project." This pro ject replaces three existing boilers (#1, #2 and #4 ) with a new boiler (#7) using low-NO x burner and flue gas recirculation technology to re duce NO x emissions. This reduction in NO x emissions is part of the overall reduction strateg y required under the PM 2.5 SIP. The new boiler #7 is subject to federal NSPS (40 CF R 60) Subparts A, Db and Ja, and MACT (40 CFR 63 NESHAP) Subparts A and DDDDD. The installation of the new boiler does not trigger or change the applicability of any other federal requirements at the refinery. This project does not trigger a major modification under either the federal non-attainment new source review (NNSR) or prevention of signification deteri oration (PSD) rules. As this project is part of th e overall strategy being implemented under the PM 2.5 SIP, plant-wide PTE will decrease following completion of all SIP-required RACT changes. New p lant-wide emission limits on PM 2.5 , NO x and SO 2 are required as of January 1, 2019. Although plant-wide emissions remain unchanged, it is possible to demonstrate the change in emissions based solely on comparing the existing #1 #2 and #4 boilers to new Boiler #7. Following completion of this project the change in emissions is as follows (all values in TPY): PM 10 +1.9, PM 2.5 +1.9, NO x -380.5, SO 2 -9.9 and VOC +1.4 (potential emissions of CO and H APs were never specifically estimated from the existing boilers). Installation of boiler #7 will decrease actual emissions of NO x by approximately 120 TPY. Current SIP Cap values and annual PTE will remain i n place at the following TPY amounts: PM 10 = 85, PM 2.5 = 65, NO x 766.5, SO 2 383.3, CO 991, and VOC 1,242. GeneralDescription: On September 26, 2016, Chevron Products Company sub mitted a NOI for a proposed project entitled the SLR Retrofit Project (project). This project involv es upgrading/replacing the existing Alkylation Unit at the Chevron Refinery; the new unit being "state-of- the-art" technology. The company is planning to remove the use of HF (hydrofluoric acid) and also t o remove the use of HF alkylation polymer as a fuel in the existing alkylation furnace. Specific upgrades include: replacement of feed dryers; replacement o f several reactor and regenerator vessels, along with various pumps and piping components; replacement o f the propane purification column; changes to other p rocess columns; installation of a new reactor vesse l, additive pressure vessel, and addition/revision of various heat exchangers. Changes will also be made to a downstream (#3) cooling tower to increase capacit y and improve efficiency. An unrelated project to incorporate reformer compre ssor emission limits required under consent decree (DOJ Case No. 90-5-2-1-07639, Civil Action No. 2:13 -cv-00721-EJF) will also be included in this permitting action, as it addresses the same underly ing AO. However, both the SLR retrofit and the compressor limit projects can be incorporated into the same AO modification and processed simultaneously. This combined project does not tri gger a major modification under either the federal non-attainment new source review (NNSR) or preventi on of signification deterioration (PSD) regulations . Emissions of some pollutants will increase slightly as a result of increased combustion of refinery fu el gas and increased utilization of process equipment. T otal emission increases in tons per year are estima ted at the following: PM 10 +1, PM 2.5 +1, NO x +7, CO +6, and VOC +24. While there is an expected increase in both potenti al (and actual) emissions from the new alkylation u nit versus the old unit, these emissions increases are for the unit itself and do not represent an increas e to Chevron's daily or annual site-wide emission Caps a s established in the SIP in sections IX.H.2 (PM 10 ) and IX.H.12 (PM 2.5 ). Current SIP Cap values and annual PTE will thus rem ain in place at the following tpy amounts: PM 10 = 261 (annual PTE), PM 2.5 (a subset of PM 10 ) = 110 (SIP Cap), NO x = 766.5 (SIP Cap), SO 2 = 383.3 (SIP Cap), CO = 991 (annual PTE), and VOC = 1,242 (annua l PTE). Total GHG emissions are estimated at 988,625 tons/yr as CO 2e. This air quality AO authorizes the project with the following conditions and failure to comply with an y of the conditions may constitute a violation of this o rder. GeneralDescription: On July 23, 2013, Chevron Products Company (Chevron ) submitted a NOI to make changes to the existing railcar loading/unloading rack (loading ra ck) at its Salt Lake Refinery. The project involve s the installation of a new vapor combustion unit as well as the installation of new pumps and piping for delivery of crude oil at the loading rack. Although the project will upgrade the loading rack to allow for the loading of crude oil, the project will not alter the refinery's total crude processing cap acity. The expected emission increases from this p roject are less than significant, and no offsets will be r equired. The total expected emission increase from the project will be (all values in tpy): PM 10 /PM 2.5 = 0.5, NO x = 2.6, SO 2 = 1.4, CO = 6.4, VOC = 12.4 and HAPs less than 10 lb/year. GHG emissions measured as CO 2e = 8,552.2 tpy. These emissions primarily come from the vapor combustor unit, although approx imately half of the VOC emissions are fugitive emissions from the storage tanks and other equipmen t. Although subject to several specific federal requir ements (NSPS and NESHAP/MACT) only a single new NESHAP requirement is triggered by this project. T he control requirements of 40 CFR 63.2346(b) and (c) as found in 40 CFR 63 Subpart EEEE apply to the loading rack and the piping connecting the loading rack to the storage tanks. No other components at the refinery are affected by this Subpart. This is a minor modification under both the nonatta inment area NSR and PSD permitting programs. Chevron's Salt Lake Refinery is defined as a major contributing source in the Salt Lake County PM 10 SIP, as well as in the Salt Lake City PM 2.5 NAA. Chevron is not increasing its existing PM 10 , PM 2.5 , SO 2 or NO x emission Caps, which will remain at the following levels: 0.234 tons/day and 85 tons/yr of PM 10 , 0.18 tons/day and 65 tons/yr of PM 2.5 , 2.1 tons/day and 766.5 tons/yr of NO x, and 1.05 tons/day and 383.3 tons/yr of SO 2. Project_Description: Hill AFB uses boilers to provide steam for base processes and comfort heating. These boilers have a range of duties from backup load (used only for a few hours per year) to nearly full -time operation. The primary fuel used for the majority of the boilers is natur al gas. Some boilers can burn more than one (1) fuel oil, and others burn only fuel oil. The alternative fuels that are currently used on base include: #2 fuel oil, diesel fuel, reclaimed jet fuel , and used oil. There are three (3) centralized boiler plants that provide heat to many other locations, and there are building or processing specific boilers. The permitted boilers have capacities ranging from 3 MMBtu per hour to 87.5 MMBtu per hour heat input. Hill AFB has no boilers ex ceeding 100 MMBtu/hr capacity so 40 CFR 60 Subpart Db does not apply. The boilers at Hill AFB are categorized into the following groups: boilers not subject to 40 CFR60 Subpart Dc, boilers with ULNO x/equivalent controls not subject to 40 CFR 60 Subpart Dc , boilers subject to 40 CFR 60 Subpart Dc, boilers with ULNO x/equivalent controls subject to 40 CFR 60 Subpart Dc, boilers that will be used for the 2023 -2024 heating season and will then be removed , and exempt boilers that meet UAC R307 -401-10 source cate gory exemption. Hill AFB has requested to replace eleven (11) boilers located in buildings 260 and 1286 with seven (7) boilers, incorporate five (5) (8.2 MMBtu/hr each) boilers which were permitted as a R307 -401-12 'Reduction in Air Pollutants ,' remove bo ilers that have been removed from Hill AFB. Hill AFB has also requested to add ammonia emissions, correct CO 2e emissions and update the opacity monitoring requirements associated to operating the boilers on a fuel other than natural gas. Operation on an alternate fuel will occur during periods of natural gas curtailment or supply interruption and for testing and maintenance. These activities may occur at night, when it is not possible to conduct an opacity survey in accordance with the requirements of 40 CFR 60, Appendix A, Method 9. 40 CFR 63 Subpart DDDDD limits operation of natural gas boilers to utilize alternate fuels to maintenance or in the event of fuel curtailment or supply interruption, by limiting operations to these specific operating conditi ons. Three (3) existing boilers are being removed to comply with Condition H.12.q.ii.B from Section IX, Part H of the Utah State Implementation Plan.

Project_Description: Hill AFB has requested an approval order for an automated fluorescent penetrant non -destructive inspection (NDI) process. The NDI process line is an auto -conveyance fluorescent penetrant system comprised of automated cycle processing stations. The line u ses three (3) chemicals: penetrant, emulsifier and developer. The penetrant and emulsifier contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs), the developer does not contain VOCs. None of the chemicals used in the process contain hazardous air pollutants (HAPs). Aircraft parts are conveyed into the enclosed penetrant station where the penetrant is  sprayed onto the part. Penetrant spray that does not coat the part is collected and drained into a waste container. After a water rinse, the part is conveyed to the e mulsifier station where it is dipped into a 700 - gallon emulsifier tank and then conveyed to a second water rinse station. The part is then conveyed to the developer station and dipped into a 700 -gallon developer tank. After drying, the part is inspected for cracks and defects.
