Project_Description: BWO submitted a NOI on May 1, 2018 for the installa tion of five (5) diesel-fired firewater pumps at it s North Salt Lake Refinery. That NOI was amended on F ebruary 13, 2019, with an additional request to create a new AO to address specific stationary sour ces at the refinery. On May 16, 2019, BWO submitted a second addendum to install an additional diesel e ngine to serve as an emergency back-up generator fo r the firewater pumps. Operating hours for maintenanc e, and engine testing purposes will not exceed 100 hours per year per engine. Therefore, this project establishes a new permitting document which is to b e used in conjunction with BWO's Process Unit AO (at the time of this document's issuance: DAQE- AN101220066-15, aka the main refinery AO) and the S torage Tanks AO (also at the time of this document's issuance: DAQE-AN101220067-16). One item currently listed in the main refinery AO - II.A.22 Standby (Emergency) Fire Pump is also being moved from that AO for inclusion in this document. BWO has submitted a separate permitting action request to update the main refinery AO which will be processed independently. Therefore, this document will cover terms and conditions for t he existing standby fire pump, new firewater pump engi nes and the new emergency back-up generator. The new firewater pump engines are 526 hp, Tier 3 units . The new emergency generator is a 96 hp, Tier 3 unit. All of the engines are fired on ultra-low su lfur diesel.

Project_Description: BWO submitted a NOI to move and update the North Fl are. The project consists of constructing a new flare in the southwest quadrant of the refinery, a greater distance from the main processing systems, tank farm and personnel. The new flare will be named th e "West Flare", given its location and to distingui sh it from the other existing "South Flare." Upon comple tion of the installation of the West Flare, the Nor th Flare will be demolished. BWO does not anticipate an increase in feed rates nor any increase in emissions from the relocation of the flare. The UDAQ is taking this opportunity to update sever al conditions of the AO to incorporate changes to Sections IX.H.1, 2, 11 and 12 of the Utah SIP that address controls on particulate matter (both PM 10 and PM 2.5 ). These changes were adopted by the Utah Air Qual ity Board on January 2, 2019. Various conditions related to the SIP Caps were updated wit h the latest revisions to the SIP and need to be updated within BWO's AO for consistency and clarity .

MACT 40 CFR 63 Subparts A & CC regulations apply to this source. Specifically, 40 CFR 60 Subpart Kb applies to Tank 58. Title V of the 1990 Clean Ai r Act applies to this source. The above-referenced project has been evaluated and found to be consistent with the requirements of UAC R307, and the Utah Air Conservation Act. A pub lic comment period was not required for this project. This air quality AO authorizes the projec t with the following conditions and failure to comp ly with any of the conditions may constitute a violati on of this order. existing HF A LKY unit with a new technology that uses an ionic liquid catalyst and involves changes to the existing ALKY unit to upgrade to state -of-the-art process technology. Specific upgrades include new feed conditioning equipment, a new ALKY reactor and settler, new ionic liquid catalyst regeneration equipment, process refrigeration equipment, replacement of the deisobutanizer distillation column, and new product treatment equipment. Various pumps, piping, and heat exchangers are to be replaced or upgraded. A press ure swing adsorption unit will be added to the refinery's catalytic reformer to purify the hydrogen feed for the new plant. The project will not affect the refinery's crude processing capacity. It will not result in significant net emissions increases , and it does not constitute a major modification. No changes to the refinery emissions caps are proposed. On August 2, 2023, BWO elected to combine the Iso -Alky Revamp project with a second project to replace Boilers 1, 2 , and 6 with three (3) new boilers . As this boiler project affects the same three (3) boilers included in the Revamp, emissions have been recalculated. The boiler replacement involves the installation of three (3) new boilers to replace boilers 1, 2 , and 6. The existing boilers are eith er at or nearing the end of life. The three (3) proposed boilers, each with a heat input capacity of 99 million British thermal units per hour ("MMBtu/hr"), higher heating value basis, will provide the plant with enough capacity for current and future nee ds.

Project_Description: On November 17, 2020, HollyFrontier Woods Cross Refining, LLC (HollyFrontier) received a determination from the USEPA establishing new 365 -day and 7 -day NO x limits fo r the Unit 4 FCCU. HollyFrontier requests these limits be incorporated into its current Approval Order, DAQE - AN101230052 -20, so as to satisfy paragraph 137(b) of its existing consent decree. HollyFrontier requests the NO x limits for FCC Unit 4 ( 4V82 FCC Scrubber) in II.B.8.b. of the Approval Order to be incorporated as follows: • The listed limit of 40 ppmvd at 0% O 2 per 365 -day rolling average changed to 22.5 ppmvd • The listed limit of 80 ppmvd at 0% O 2 per seven -day rolling average changed to 40 ppmvd In a separate request, HollyFrontier submitted a request (dated April 1, 2022) to update the equipment list to reflect various changes in installed equipment, the removal of non -emission point equipment, and include equipment specifically requested by UDAQ. These changes are summarized as follows:  • Tank 169, with a capacity of 750 barrels, will replace existing Tank 88, which has a capacity of 26,730 barrels • Cooling Tower 11 will be modified to h ave an expanded recirculation rate rather than adding a second cell • An SRU backup scrubber will be added and designated as Unit 29 • Several chemical storage tanks with no emissions will be removed from the equipment list

GeneralDescription: Holly Refining & Marketing Company - Woods Cross LL C (Holly) is requesting to install and operate three (3) dedicated ethanol unloading areas in orde r to support the EPA's renewable fuel standard by blending produced gasoline to a 10% ethanol concent ration. The Holly refinery is located in West Bountiful, Davis County. Davis County is nonattain ment for PM 2.5 and is a maintenance area for Ozone. Holly is located four miles north of Salt Lake Coun ty and is defined as a contributing source for the Salt Lake County PM 10 nonattainment area. Holly is a major source of HA Ps, a SIP source, and a PSD source; however, this modification does not trigger PSD review. NSPS and NESHAP regulations apply to this source. GeneralDescription: On June 11, 2015, Tesoro Refining & Marketing Compa ny LLC (Tesoro) submitted a NOI to construct and operate a wet gas scrubber and LoTOx project to control emissions from the FCCU/CO Boiler at the Tesoro Refinery in Salt Lake City (Refinery). The Refinery is located in Salt Lake City, Salt Lake County, which is a nonattainment area for PM 10 , PM 2.5 and SO 2, and a maintenance area for ozone and CO. This project was submitted in partial fulfillm ent of the PM 2.5 requirements found in Sections IX.H.11.g.i and IX.H.12.r of the Utah SIP. The project involves the installation of an ozone g eneration system to react with and remove NOx from the FCCU/CO boiler exhaust stream. The exhaust gas es are then passed through a new spray tower, which removes the NOx compounds, as well as SO 2/SO 3 and particulates. The removed compounds are retained in the spray tower's liquid medium - water , buffered with a sodium hydroxide reagent. The liquid is then processed in a purge treatment unit, which separates and dewaters the solids. This res ults in a final effluent stream that is low in total suspen ded solids. Although there will be a small increase in fugitive dust emissions from increased truck traffic to del iver raw materials and haul away waste byproducts associ ated with the project, no change in plant-wide particulate emissions is anticipated as a result of this project. This project was supplemented on October 12, 2015, with the submission of additional information. Tesoro determined that approximately 17,500 lb/hr o f additional steam generation would be needed to drive the combustion air fan, providing the higher pressures needed in the flue gas train. There woul d be a slight increase in actual emissions from the exis ting cogeneration units at the refinery, the larges t being approximately 6 TPY of NOx, although this increase would be within Tesoro’s existing permitted emission caps. Tesoro also provided information re garding truck haul road emissions as part of this submission. The fugitive dust emissions from the h aul roads amount to less than 0.1 tons of total particulates (either PM 10 or PM 2.5 ). Finally, on November 10, 2015, Tesoro submitted a s eparate NOI for the installation of ultra-low NOx burners on the ultraformer unit (UFU) F-1 furnace. Also submitted in partial fulfillment of the PM 2.5 requirements of the Utah SIP, this project is sched uled to be installed at roughly the same time as th e wet gas scrubber project. GeneralDescription: Tesoro Refining and Marketing Company (Tesoro) owns and operates a refinery located in Salt Lake City. This refinery is a PM 10 SIP listed source. The Tesoro Refinery is located in Salt Lake County, which is a nonattainment area of the NAAQS for PM 10, PM 2.5 (a subset of PM 10) and SO 2. The refinery is also located in a maintenance area for ozone and CO. Tesoro has submitted two NOIs for separate and unrelated projects at the refinery. Since both projects are expected to begin construction at the same time Tesoro has requested that these proj ects be combined into one permitting effort resulting in only one new AO. The first project is called the CONO x system, which involves injecting oxygen into the FCCU regenerator offgas duct. This will allow for selective combustion of CO to CO 2 while also improving efficiency at the CO boiler. The second project is called the LPG Recovery Project. When LPG products are unloaded into sealed tanks at the refinery, vaporiz ed propane is used to evacuate the rail cars and trucks. The new system will also be able to use butanes for this process. GeneralDescription: Kennecott Utah Copper LLC (KUC) submitted an NOI pr oposing a crushed stone processing plant. The product from the new crushed stone processing plant , Bonneville Borrow Plant (BBP), will be used to supply building materials related to the constructi on of the proposed expansion of the Tailings site. Placement of the tailings cannot occur until the dr ainage blankets and finger drains are constructed. This allows for a better compaction of the mechanically placed tailings. The BBP will be a new crushed sto ne processing plant, specifically, a crushing and scre ening plant. The plant will be located west of Mag na, Utah. The crushed material will be hauled to the T ailings site on KUC roads and will not be hauled on public roads. This AO is for the crushing and proc essing of the material and associated equipment necessary to perform this activity at the BBP. Thi s will be the second public comment period for this NOI. The first public comment period was held from January 10, 2012 to February 10, 2012 in which comments were received that resulted in new conditi ons and requirements being added to the proposed BBP AO. Due to the lengthy delay since the first c omment period, a second comment period will be held to enable the public the opportunity to review the new ITA. NSPS, Subpart OOO (Standards of Performance for Non metallic Mineral Processing Plants) applies to the BBP. The BBP is a minor source. The emissions are predominately fugitive. The BBP is located in Salt Lake County which is a nonattainment area of t he NAAQS for PM 10 , PM 2.5 and SO 2, and is a maintenance area for ozone. Title V of the 1990 Cl ean Air Act applies to this source. The PM 10 emissions will be offset at a 1.2:1 ratio. GeneralDescription: On December 15, 2010 Kennecott Utah Copper, LLC (KU C) submitted a NOI to install and operate a new combined-cycle, natural gas-fired combustion tu rbine (CT) to replace three existing coal-fired boi lers (identified as Units 1, 2 and 3 boilers). The facility-wide potential to emit totals following the installation of the new CT and after the shut-down of Units 1, 2 and 3 boilers are as follows (again in TPY): PM 10 = 248, PM 2.5 = 248, NO x = 1,641, SO 2 = 2,577, CO = 328, VOC = 41 and total HAPs = 9. The total PTE emissions change from the permit combination, two (2) new operations, and the updated emissions and permit equipment result i n a reduction in emissions. Other permit changes consist of replacing equipment and are as follows: Boiler Replacement The Wickes Boiler Co. natural gas -fired boiler (71.1 MMBtu/hr) has failed and is now being replaced with a Nationwide natural gas -fired boiler (65.5 MMBtu/hr) at Building M576. The new boiler has been upgraded with an anhydrous ammonia -based SCR system to achieve 9 ppm NOx emissions. The replacement of the boiler has an overall reduction of emissions ( -4.44 TPY NOx and -10.65 TPY CO). Removal of Portable Boilers NGSC has requested to remove two (2) portable oil -fired boilers (50 hp and 100 hp). The estimated emissions calculations utilize AP -42, Chapter 1.3 (Tables 1.3 -1 and 1.3 -3), and EPA WebFire emission factors. All emissions are re duced. Sulfur Content In review of the permits, NGSC identified the AO DAQE -AN160230005 -23 sulfur estimated emissions used legacy SO2 emissions (at 0.85 lb S/MMBtu) and have now been updated with new SO2 emissions (at 0.0015 percent S).  Dust Collectors Two (2) dust collectors were previously removed from the approval order, but the PTE was not adjusted to account for the removals. The removal of the dust collectors has a reduction of 5.71 TPY of both PM 10 and PM 2.5. The dust collector in building E -517 is being vented outside, which was initially internally vented. A new dust collector will be added to Building M -348. The result of venting the dust collector outside increases the PM 10 (3.32 TPY) and PM 2.5 (3.82 TPY) emissions. The dust collector in M -348 increases the PM 10 and PM 2.5 by 0.01 TPY, each.

Project_Description: NGSC has requested to install two (2) new operations (Medium Composite -Wound solid rocket Motor (MCM) and the Small Composite -Wound solid rocket Motor (SCM)) at the Promontory site. These new operations will include operational emissions and new process eq uipment emissions that will be included in the process equipment AO. The operational MCM emissions consist of winding and liner preparation operations in building M348 and the assembly operations in buildings M622 and M627. The operational SCM emissions co nsist of propellant premix in building M012 case preparation in building M369 and the final assembly in building M702. The permit change involves the combination of two (2) permits: AO DAQE -AN100090124 -14 (Testing of Propellants, Explosives , and Pyrotech nics) and AO DAQE -012-00 (Production of Energetic Material). The PTE for both permits has been combined. The MCM and SCM operation emissions have been included along with the emissions reductions from updated emissions from the two (2) permits. The total PTE emissions change from the permit combination, 2 new operations , and the updated permit emissions result s in a reduction in VOC and HAP emissions. This permit change is being conducted as an Administrative Amendment per UAC R307 -401-12, Reduction of Air Pollutants. Composite -Wound Motors The new composite -wound solid rocket motor production is broken down into two groups : MCM and SCM. The MCM manufacturing line includes filament winding of carbon fiber cases, case lining, propellant mixing and casting, and assembly of the final rocket motor. The SCM manufacturing line includes case lining, propellant mixing and casting, and assembly of the final rocket motor. Existing propellant mixing and casting facilities will be used for MCM and SCM production. Testing Operations NGSC currently operates two (2) testing sites in Box Elder County. One site is located at the Promontory site (Lampo Junctio n), and one is located at the KOSMO test site (Salt Wells Flat, AO DAQE -AN148540001 -14). The Promontory site testi ng operations are separated into two (2) categories : small and large testing operations (T24 and T97 testing sites, AO DAQE -AN160230006 -24). This permit includes small testing operations. The testing operations emissions included in this AO have an emissio ns decrease of -3.67 TPY of SO x emissions due to the sulfur content of the fuels being updated to 0.0015 percent. Energetic Operations NGSC produces various energetic materials for use in explosives manufacturing. Building M -590 is used to conduct process research from bench -top to pilot -scale to production of energetics. Energetic materials are produced using a process where a feed stock, usually obtained from off -site, is nitrated using an acid mixture. This nitration process emits NO x. Some of the energe tic manufacturing processes emit HAPs. This process includes the use of four (4) nitration process reactors and a scrubber. The reactors have been included in this AO, but the scrubber is included in the process equipment AO. This permit change includes a n emissions reduction of -13 TPY of Diethanolamine and a -15 TPY of VOC. The emissions reductions reflect actual manufacturing emission rates.

Project_Description: Northrop Grumman Systems Corporation (formerly ATK Launch Systems, LLC) has requested a modification to AO DAQE -AN0009105 -05 to increase the daily rocket motor test limit to develop a new generation of large solid rocket motor s for NASA's Booster Obsolescence and Life Extension (BOLE) program. The new rocket motor will exceed the daily test limit of 700 tons of propellant at the Promontory fa cility's T -97 test stand. Northrop Grumman Systems Corporation is requesting approval to increase the propellant limitation on the T -97 test stand from 700 tons per test to 750 tons. The propellant mass of the new motor will be above the existing daily t est limit. Annual mass limitations for BOLE motor testing will decrease from 2,100 tons to 1,500 tons due to a decrease in the number of rocket motor tests per year ( from three (3) tests per year to two (2) tests per year). The e mission factors used for permitting the solid rocket motor in 2001 were reviewed to determine the applicability of those previously used and the availability of new emission factors. A review of PM 10 and PM 2.5 emission factors was conducted. The best data available related to pa rticulate matter in the exhaust of solid rocket motor tests was generated in 1996. New drone and sensor technology developed since the 1996 study has developed updated emission estimates for solid rocket motor testing. The updated emissions factors will be incorporated into this permit change. PM 2.5, SO x, and lead were not addressed in the 2001 permit. In determining the project type, the existing rocket motor test operations were recalculated using updated emission factors. The updated emissions from the existing operation (using the new emission factors) are assumed to be existing and not taken into consideration in this modification.

GeneralDescription: Solar Temp Fiberglass Inc orporated (Solar Temp) is a fiberglass composite manufacturing plant located in Woods Cross, Davis County. Approved equipment on site consists of one (1) gelcoater, one (1) chopper gun, one (1) grinder, and two (2) natural gas comfort heating units. To create the f iberglass composite products, Solar Temp begins by spraying a mold with gelcoat (paint). When the gelcoat is dry, the mold is sprayed with the chopper gun and a resin mixture. Excess material is gr ound off, and the product is ready for shipment.

Applicable_Federal_Standards: NSPS (Part 60), A: General Provisions NSPS (Part 60), Dc: Standards of Performance for Small Industrial -Commercial -Institutional Steam Generating Units NSPS (Part 60), I: Standards of Performance for Hot Mix Asphalt Facilities NSPS (Part 60), OOO: Standards of Performance for Nonmetallic Mineral Processing Plants MACT (Part 63), A: General Provisions MACT (Part 63), CCCCCC: National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants for DAQE -AN112340015 -24 Page 4 Source Category: Gasoline Dispensing Facilities Title V (Part 70) Area Source Project Proposal Administrative Amendment to Approval Order DAQE -AN112340013 -17 to Replace One (1) Hot Mix Asphalt Plant and Associated Equipm ent and Update Potential to Emit (PTE) under R307 -401-12.
Project_Description: Staker has requested the following changes: 1. Replace and relocate one ( 1) drum mix asphalt plant and associated baghouse (currently listed as Equipment II.A.19 and II.A.20 in Approval Order DAQE -AN112340013 -17). 2. Increase the number of asphalt cement storage tanks (Equipment II.A.23 in the 2017 AO) from three (3) to six (6 ). 3. Replace four (4) uncontrolled asphalt storage silos (Equipment II.A.24 in the 2017 AO) with six (6) new blue smoke -controlled asphalt storage silos . 4. Add one (1) hot oil heater rated at 2.8 MMBtu/hr . 5. Add two (2) 12,000 -gallon diesel fuel stor age tanks . 6. Replace one (1) 900 -gallon gasoline fuel storage tank (Equipment II.A.28 in the 2017 AO) with one (1) 2,000 -gallon gasoline fuel storage tank. 7. Remove three (3) diesel generators (currently listed as Equipment II.A.13 and II.A.14 in Approva l Order DAQE -AN112340013 -17). 8. Remove one (1) fuel -oil-fired, 7 MMBtu/hr boiler (Equipment II.A.29 in the 2017 AO) . 9. Remove all used fuel -oil-related conditions, as the facility is discontinuing use of used fuel -oil. Additionally, Staker has reques ted that a calculation error in the 2017 AO regarding the PTE be corrected. The application for the current 2017 AO was evaluated in 2016 and contained several errors. In the 2016 application, emissions from hot mix asphalt (HMA) operations were estimated using only emission s from the drum mixer and did not take into consideration emissions from the HMA silos, cement storage tanks, hot oil system, asphalt loadout, lime silos, etc. Additionally, no emissions were calculated for the 7 MMBtu/hr Kemco diesel -fired boiler nor the gasoline and diesel fuel storage tanks. Staker has elected to remedy this oversight by re -evaluating the site -wide PTEs . Had the 2016 emission estimates been done correctly and accounted for all the emission units, all actual PTEs would be decreasing. Additionally, the new HMA plant will only be permitted to operate on natural gas. The current HMA is permitted to run on fuel oil and used oil in addition to natural gas. Limiting the fuel type to natural gas, which burns cleaner, contributes to a decrease in all PTEs. This project meets the requirements of UAC R307 -401-12 Reduction in Air Pollutants and does not require a pu blic comment period.

The pit has several feeders, crushers, and screens that produce PM emissions. The site also has wash plants, a bag pla nt, and the concrete plant, which includes cement s ilos and a water heater. The site is currently permitted to produce 1,500,000 tons of aggregate and 225,000 cubic yards of concrete. Staker & Parson Companies employs several pollution control techniques. Baghouses and bin vents to reduce PM emissions. Low NO x burners reduce NO x from a water heater. Using natural gas fuel will reduce SO x, PM, and VOC emissions.

Project_Description: Staker & Parson Companies requested a clarification on compliance language for conditions II.B.4.b.1 and II.B.4.d.1. Conditions II.B.4.b.1 and II.B.4.d.1 limit total acreage that can be occupied by storage piles and disturbed ground and the total length of unpave d haul roads. In order to achieve compliance with these limits, the source must periodically monitor the total area occupied by the storage piles, disturbed ground, and haul road length. Due to a clerical error, the agreed -upon language for these complianc e conditions was not incorporated into the final AO that was issued. This amendment updates conditions II.B.4.b.1 and II.B.4.d.1 to reflect the correct required monitoring frequency. Stack testing requirements additionally included both condensable and fil terable particulate matter in the definition for total PM 10 and PM 2.5. This is incorrect for hot mix asphalt plants , as condensable emissions are not normally required for stack testing at these operations. The definitions in II.B.5.j.2 and II.B.5.j.3 ha ve been changed to reflect the intended method of compliance with the limit in II.B.5.h. Finally, the equipment list has been updated due to an error in the request for feed bins ; the permit was originally issued for four ( 4) bins, but the site actually has seven ( 7). Condition II.A.9 has been changed to reflect this.

SourceName: Staker Parson Companies - Keigley Quarry(Site ID: 12444)
GeneralDescription: Staker Parson Companies operates an aggregate and asphalt processing plant at its Keigley Quarry in Utah County. The aggregate processing plant includes crushers, screens, conveyors/stackers, feeders, and loaders. The aspha lt plant is equipped with a hot -mix asphalt plant and associated baghouse, storage silos and bins, a wash plant, a feeder, and support equipment including oil heaters, conveyors, loaders, bulldozers, and holding tanks. The dust mill includes a feeder, a ho pper, a grinder, a furnace, silos, and dust collectors. Annual production is limited to 6 million tons of aggregate, 0.5 million tons of asphalt, and 0.1 million tons of rock dust.

Project_Description: Staker Parson Companies has proposed to: 1. Add a dust mill to the Keigley Quarry. Processed aggregate will be conveyed and transferred to a grinder , where the aggregate i s pulverized into rock dust. The rock dust is flash -dried by a furnace and then transferred to a storage silo. The rock dust is then conveyed to a fully enclosed bagging operation. The bagged rock dust will then be shipped out to customers. The source pro poses to produce 100,000 tons of rock dust per year. The emissions from the dust mill will be added to the site ’s total PTE. 2. Add drilling and blasting operations to the Keigley Quarry. The source proposes to conduct 24 blasts a year and use 20 tons of ANFO per blast. The emissions from the drilling and blasting operations will be added to the site ’s total PTE. 3. Add three (3) fuel storage tanks. The source proposes to install one (1) 10,000 -gallon diesel fuel tank, one (1) 4,000 -gallon diesel fuel t ank, and one (1) 1,000 -gallon gasoline tank. The emissions from the new fuel storage tanks will be added to the site ’s total PTE.

Project_Description: Staker is requesting a modification to AO DAQE -AN120850002 -23, issued July 20, 2023, to increase the allowable hours of operation. Per the 2023 AO, Condition II.B.1.c, Staker is only permitted to operate the Lehi-Peck Pit between 6:00 am and 10:00 pm each day. Staker is requesting to increase the allowable  operating hours of the concrete batch plant to 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and 52 weeks a year. Staker is also requesting to update the hourly throughput of the aggregate processing plant from 1,200 to ns per hour (tpy) to 800 tph and to replace the 9.9 MMBtu/hr, natural gas -fired water heater (Equipment ID II.A.9 in the 2023 AO) with a 7.0 MMBtu/hr, natural gas -fired water heater. Emissions from aggregate processing are calculated based on annual prod uction. Because annual production is not increasing, no additional annual emissions are emitted from increasing the hours of operation. However, replacing the water heater with one of a lower rating will result in decreased emissions. Therefore, the reques ted changes will decrease the site -wide annual potential to emit (PTE) of the facility. Because Staker has requested to increase the allowable hours of operation, this project will be processed as a modification.

SourceName: Sunnyside Cogeneration Associates-  Sunnyside Cogeneration Facility(Site ID: 10096)
GeneralDescription: On April 4, 2012, Sunnyside Cogeneration Associates (SCA) submitted a NOI to construct and operate a new ash landfill at SCA's cogeneration facility loc ated in Sunnyside, Carbon County. The existing ash landfill will be closed prior to f ull-time operation of the new landfill; however, du ring the transition period before complete closure of th e existing landfill, Sunnyside will only be placing ash in one landfill at a time. SCA has imposed limits on haul road speed, as well as implementing vigorous watering and dust suppression techniques. In addit ion, the haul road to the new landfill is shorter. Therefore, future actual emissions from landfilling the ash are expected to decrease slightly. Howeve r, this change is negligible, resulting in a total exp ected decrease of 3.3 tons of fugitive PM 10 emissions and a decrease of 0.33 tons of PM 2.5 (a subset of PM 10 ). This represents a small fraction of SCA's total particulate emissions. Plant-wide emissions are es timated as follows: 139.61 tpy of PM 10 1,289.26 tpy of SO 2 771.2 tpy of NO x 261.96 tpy of CO 18.17 tpy of VOC The plant and landfill are located in Carbon County which is an attainment area of the NAAQS for all pollutants. SCA is a Title V Major Source operatin g under Operating Permit #700030002. R307-401-12: Reduction in Air Contaminants applies to this permi t change, SCA requested that this change be include d in its Title V renewal application. This AO change addresses that request. The AO will also be updated to correctly identify t he plant as subject to 40 CFR 60 Subpart Da. In 20 07, US EPA changed the applicability requirements for N SPS Subparts Da and Db, causing the main boiler to switch from Subpart Db to being subject to Subpart Da. The limitations and requirements will be updat ed as appropriate. No other changes will be made. SourceName: Sunroc Corporation - Ft. Pierce Pit(Site ID: 12516)
GeneralDescription: Sunroc Corporation (Sunroc), located in the F ort Pierce area in St. George, Washington County, operates aggregate, concrete batching, and hot mix asphalt operations at its Fort Pierce Pit. Aggregate is harvested via drilling and blasting of the rock formations and then fed into crushers and screens. Aggregates cycle through the crushers and screens to achieve appropriate sizing and are stored on location before being trucked off site or to the asphalt plant or concrete batch plant. The asphalt plant mixes aggregate, asphalt oil, lime, and fly ash to p roduce hot mix asphalt. The concrete batch plant mixes cement, fly ash, and aggregate to create ready -mix concrete. The manufactured asphalt and concrete are trucked to customers. Sunroc uses bulldozers, front -end loaders, aggregate haul trucks, hot mix asphalt trucks, and concrete delivery trucks in its production process. The plant produces up to 600,000 tons of aggregate materials, 405,000 tons of hot mix asphalt product, and 225,000 cubic yards of concrete per year.

GeneralDescription: Sync Aggregate has requested an AO for a portable source. This AO is issued to Sync Aggregate for the purpose of operating a portable source that belongs to the nonmetallic mineral processing industry, including aggregate, concrete, and hot mix asphalt pl ants. The plants subject to this AO shall be temporarily operated for a period of not more than 180 working days at any location. A relocation shall not exceed 365 consecutive days at any location in the state of Utah. Prior to commencement of operation at a location, the owner/operator shall submit a Noti ce of Temporary Relocation to the Director and obtain a Temporary Relocation Approval Letter. If the owner/operator operates at a location in compliance with the AO, the Notice of Temporary Relocation, and the Temporary Relocation Approval Letter, dispers ion modeling results have determined that there will be no adverse impacts on air quality at the nearest residence or commercial establishment. Compliance with the opacity limits and operating practices contained in the conditions of the AO shall be considered as application of BACT. The emission control measures required in the conditions of this AO shall apply to all of the locations at which the source approved by this AO operates. The source may be required to adopt additional measures for controll ing emissions to address location -specific concerns. This AO limits the source to emissions that are below the major source threshold, making it a "Synthetic Minor" source.

The emission control measures that the owner/operator proposes to ad opt for each emission point at each location; including a FDCP specific to the proposed location G. A reference to this AO. [R307 -401-8] II.B.2 Temporary Relocation Approval Letter: II.B.2.a The owner/operator shall operate and conduct its operation s of the aggregate processing plants, the concrete batch plants, the hot mix asphalt plants, and the diesel -fired engines in accordance with the terms and conditions of this AO and the terms and conditions of the Temporary Relocation Approval Letter issued by the Director for each relocation. [R307 -401-8] II.B.3 Operations at Temporary Locations: II.B.3.a The owner/operator may tempo rarily relocate this portable source to any temporary location. SourceName: TCR Composites Inc.(Site ID: 12101)
GeneralDescription: TCR Composites Inc. is an advanced composite materials manufacturer specializing in thermoset epoxy resins and custom pre -impregnated (prepreg) composite materials. These products are used in an array of industries , including aerospace, automotive, industrial, and marine. The facility is located in Weber County. The process starts with roving and woven manmade fibers (carbon, glass, aramid) that are purchased from third party suppliers. The roving and woven materials are then impregnated with TRC composites' custom two-part epoxy -resin systems according to customer needs. After impregnation, the prepreg is sent through a process that varies in temperature and time based on the resin system before being shipped to customers. Following the manufacturing processes, all impregnation equipment is cleaned with a 50:50 cleaning solvent blend of MEK and acetone.

TOC Oil Inc. - Bridger Lake Plant(Site ID: 10679)
GeneralDescription: TOC Oil Inc. has an AO to remediate contaminated soil and groundwater at the Bridger Lake Compressor Station. Remediation technologies that are employed include total fluids recovery, soil vapor extraction, and air sparging of in situ groundwater. Extracted groundwater and free-phase hydrocarbons from the total fluids recovery are separated, and groundwater is hauled on site to an injection well for disposal, and free product is trucked off site. Emissions from all of these activities will be vented to the atmosphere. The source is located in Summit County, which is an attainment area of the NAAQS for all pollutants. NSPS, NESHAP, and MACT regulations do not apply to this source. Title V of the 1990 Clean Air Act does not apply to this source.

SourceName: TTM Technologies, Inc.- Logan Circuit Board Plant(Site ID: 10056)
GeneralDescription: TTM Technologies, Inc. (TTM) is the owner/operator of the Logan Circuit Board Plant in Logan, Cache Count y. The process starts with a blank circuit board. Circuit board designs are printed or developed onto films. These films are used to create a map for physical etching and chemical stripping into the board to create the necessary pathways and attachments. P rocess baths are used to accomplish the chemical component of this process. Masks are rolled on by a machine to protect the board and then sealed through electric oven heating. The Drilling and Routing line is used to sketch additional design pathways onto the board. The board is then dipped into metal tanks to create electrical pathways. Individual plates are compiled into one core and then treated through a similar process in the South Building. The completed circuit panels are packaged and shipped off -site.

SourceName: Tesoro Logistics Operations LLC - Truck Loading Rack and Remote Tank Farm(Site ID: 15659)
GeneralDescription: Tesoro Logistics Operations LLC (TLO) operates a Remote Tank Farm (RTF) and Truck Loading Rack (TLR). The TLR is located on property adjacent to and south of the Tesoro Refinery, but is fenced and monitored so that authorized access is controlled independently from the refinery. The RTF is located approximately one ( 1) mile northwest of the Tesoro Refinery . The TLR is a fuel -loading facility for distribution of products produced by the Tesoro Salt Lake City Refinery. The facility includes five (5) truck transport load spots , each capable of delivering gasoline, jet fuel, and distillate products to tank tru cks. Ethanol and other additives are blended in line with refined products during transfer to trucks. Runoff from the loading bays is routed through a catch basin and then to the oil -water separator (OWS). Oil separated from the runoff is routed from the O WS to the refinery's slop oil system, and the water phase is routed to the refinery's oily water sewer. A crude oil unloading facility is also operated at the TLR. Crude oil is unloaded from tank trucks and transferred directly to the refinery or stored wi thin crude storage tanks at the TLR before being transferred to the refinery. The RTF receives crude oil via pipeline, which is stored in storage tanks before being sent to the Tesoro Refinery crude unit. The RTF also has bidirectional pipelines from the refinery and storage tanks , which are used to supplement gasoline and diesel storage capacity at the Tesoro Refinery. In addition, gasoline and diesel products are distributed to transportation pipelines from the RTF.

Sets of equipment chosen from the equipment approved in the tables at the end of this AO shall be temporarily operated for a period of not more than 180 operating days in any calendar year at any site. A relocation shall not exceed 365 consecutive days at any location in the State of Utah. Prior to commencement of operation at a site, the source shall submit a Notice of Temporary Relocation to the Executive Secretary. If the plant operates, at a site, in compliance with the AO, the Notice of Temporary Relocation, and the Temporary Relocation Approval Letter, dispersion modeling results have determined that there will be no adverse impacts on air quality at the nearest residence or commercial establishment. Compliance with the opacity limits and various operating practices listed in the conditions of the AO shall be considered as application of Best Available Control Technology. The emission control measures listed in the conditions of this AO shall apply to all of the sites at which the equipment approved by this AO operates. The source may be required to adopt additional measures for controlling emissions to address site-specific concerns. The tables at the end of this AO shall indicate whether the equipment is subject to the New Source Performance Standards, (NSPS, 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOO). The Temporary Relocation Approval Letter, which the source is required to possess prior to operation, shall list the allowable emissions and/or production limits for the relocation. This AO limits the source to emissions, which are below the major source threshold, making it a “Synthetic Minor” source. Other applicability factors may still apply. The terms and conditions of this AO are enforceable by both the State of Utah and the Federal Government. A public comment period was held in accordance with UAC R307-401-7 and no comments were received. SourceName: Uinta Wax Operating, LLC - North Randlett Compressor Station(Site ID: 15938)
GeneralDescription: Uinta Wax Operating, LLC (Uinta) operates the North Randlett Compressor Station in Uintah County. Gas enters the station where it is separated from liquids in the slug catcher. Gas leaves the slug catcher and is compressed. The liquids are sent to atmospheric tanks. During compression, liquids fall out and are sent to the three -phase separator. Water and condensate from the separator is sent to atmospheric storage tanks, NGLs are sent to bullet tanks, and gas is recycled to the station inlet. Any vapors from the atmospheric tanks are controlled by combustors. The gas then enters a hydrogen sulfide (H 2S) contact tank to strip H 2S out of the gas stream. The gas then enters the Triethylene Glycol (TEG) dehydration unit where the dry gas is recycled to the station inle t or routed to a sales line. The facility will have twelve diesel storage tanks onsite for short term storage unrelated to production at the compressor station. The diesel is used for production in the area.

GeneralDescription: ConocoPhillips Company (ConocoPhillips) conducts gas compression, carbon dioxide (CO 2) natural gas stripping, and gas dehydration operations at the C -2 compressor station in Carbon County. Low-pressure coal bed methane is sent by pipeline to the plant and is compressed to 600 -900 psi by electric compressors. After passing through the kno ckout drum to remove impurities, the gas stream is sent to the amine unit to remove CO 2. In this process, the gas is passed through an absorber where the CO 2 is absorbed by the amine solution. The CO 2-rich solution is then fed to a regenerator where the stream is heated to roughly 300°F to drive off the CO 2 from the amine. The lean amine solution is returned to the absorption tower to be reused. Once the CO 2 is removed, the feed gas passes through the glycol dehydration unit for water removal. After t he water is removed, the feed gas flows through a dry gas scrubber to remove any entrained glycol and is then combined with other dry gasses to produce sale gas.

SourceName: Urban Oil and Gas Group, LLC- Orangeville #2 Natural Gas Compressor Station(Site ID: 13077)
GeneralDescription: Buzzards Bench LLC owns and operates Orangeville #2 Natural Gas Compressor Station. This station operates two electric compressor motors. Natural gas from the gathering wells enter the inlet header and flows to the inlet scrubber, which separates the water from the gas. The gas then flows to the single -stage compressor in which filters separate the gas from the compressor ’s lubricating oil. Once compressed, the gas is cooled before flowing through the outlet scrubbers. The outlet scrubbers also filter out more water from the compressed gas before being piped to the Orangeville Compressor Station #1. The excess water separated by the scrubbers is pumped on site into a water storage tank. The water is eventually pumped from the tank to the salt water disposal well and injected back into the ground. Water may also be trucked off site in emergencies. The oil that cycles through the compressor motor is eventually transferred on site to the oil storage tank where it eventually is trucked off site.

GeneralDescription: Utah Iron, LLC (Utah Iron) , operates an iron ore mine and beneficiation plant in Iron County near Cedar City, Utah. SourceName: Ute Railway Logistics- Ute Rail Terminal(Site ID: 16280)
GeneralDescription: Ute Railway Logistics LLC (URL) conducts petroleum liquid transloading operations between railcars and trucks using intermediate storage tanks. Vapor combustion units (VCUs) are employed to control emissions from railcar loading and utilize propane gas as support fuel. The propane is stored in storage bullet (pressurized) tanks. An emergency diesel -fired engine and associated diesel fuel storage tank are installed to run firewater pumps and heating equ ipment in the event of a shutdown. The crude oil storage tanks are equipped with heaters and the piping will be equipped with heat tracing; these heating components will all be electric/non -emitting. The URL rail terminal has a maximum throughput of 41,756 ,000 bbl per year of crude oil.

Project_Description: URL has requested the AO specify the construction phases. There is an initial phase (Phase 0) where mobile transloading equipment is utilized to lo ad railcars directly from trucks, without intermediate storage tanks. Phase 0 operations require the use of a diesel generator and associated diesel fuel storage tank for primary power. The mobile transloading equipment is controlled by the use of carbon adsorber drums. All Phase 0 equipment except for the diesel fuel storage tank are to be removed from the site and not utilized in Phase 1 and Phase 2. Phase 1 is the partial development on the site, with full development occurring in Phase 2. The final site configuration for equipment on site includes equipment from Phase 1 and Phase 2. Phase 1 and 2 equipment includes crude oil and propane storage tanks, an emergency diesel -fired engine, and vapor VCUs to control fugitive emissions. The difference between Phase 1 and Phase 2 is the addition of several truck/railcar unloading spots, crude oil storage tanks, and a second vapor combustion unit (VCU) and associated propane tank.

SourceName: Utility Trailer Manufacturing Company- Trailer Manufacturing Facility(Site ID: 10156)
GeneralDescription: Utility Trailer Manufacturing Company (Utility Trailer) operates a refrigerated (climate controlled) truck trailer manufacturing operation. Utility Trailer had previously been classified as a minor source for the emission of Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) but is now considered to be a synthetic minor (SM) source. Parking areas are paved. Trailer storage areas are covered with rototilled asphalt, gravel , or soil.
Source_Classification: Located in Davis County Airs Source Size: SM
Applicable_Federal_Standards: MACT (Part 63), A: General Provisions MACT (Part 63), ZZZZ: National Emissions Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants for Stationary Reciprocating Internal Combustion Engines DAQE -AN101560020 -24 Page 4
Project_Description: Utility Trailer has requested an administrative amendment to AO DAQE -AN101560017 -17 to reduce the trailer manufactur ing facility ’s VOC PTE emissions from 69 TPY to 49 TPY. The revised PTE emissions for the trailer manufactur ing facility lower the site -wide PTEs below the major source classification for sources located in the ozone nonattainment designation in the Northern Wasatch Front nonattainment area. This permit update has no new equipment or increase in emission s, so it is not subject to UAC , R307 -401-5 and subject to UAC R307 -401-12, "Reduction in Air Pollutants ." The last three (3) years of emissions inventories were reviewed to determine the average VOC of actual emissions. The average actual VOC emissions that were sub mitted to Utah Division of Air Quality (UDAQ ) for the years 2021, 2022 , and 2023 are 33.72 TPY of VOC. This value validates that the trailer manufactur ing facility can reduce the PTE to 49 TPY with the current operations. Process Description Utility Trail er manufactures refrigerated truck trailers. Stainless steel is cut and welded together to fabricate the structural frames for the trailers. Some of the sub -assemblies for trailer frames are dipped into tanks containing corrosion inhibitors. Painting is co nducted in the large, filtered paint spray booths. Trailer shells constructed from aluminum, stainless steel, wood , and/or fiberglass are mounted onto the trailer frames. A foaming insulator mixture blended with an expansion agent is injected into the wal ls, floors, and ceiling of the trailers. Once the foam insulation cures, the trailers are moved to a final assembly area where wheels, electrical wiring, lights , and miscellaneous features (such as refrigeration units) are installed.

SourceName: Valmont Coatings, Inc. - Intermountain Galvanizing- Galvanizing Facility(Site ID: 10833)
GeneralDescription: Valmont Coating, Inc. operates a hot dip steel galvanizing and painting facility in Lindon. Steel is delivered to the facility by trucks before being unloaded by forklifts and moved into the receiving yard. At t he facility, steel can be galvanized, painted, or both. To be galvanized, steel is moved inside the building to a "racking area," where each piece is chained/attached to an overhead crane (racked) before being dipped into a series of tanks. The tanks include: two caustic soda tanks, a caustic rinse tank, four sulfuric acid descaling tanks, two sulfuric acid rinse tanks, a zinc/ammonium chloride pre -flux tank, one molten zinc kettle, and one water quench tank. The facility also operates a powder coat line where steel is blasted and painted. Finished steel products are moved to the shipping yard before being loaded onto trucks. The water used in process tanks is heated by means of two natural gas-fired boilers.
Project_Description: This is an administrative amendment to DAQE -AN108330005 -12 dated November 28, 2012. The DAQ is conducting a 10 -year review and is updating the language and format of the 2012 AO. According to a DAQ compliance inspection memorandum (see DAQC -410-24 date d March 19, 2024), the 9 MMBtu/hr kettle listed in DAQE -AN108330005 -12 was taken offline with no plans for further use. On August 23, 2024, the source verified they no longer use the 9 MMBtu/kettle and indicated the only kettle they would like included on t heir permit is the 6 MMBtu/hr kettle listed in DAQE -AN108330005 -12. The 9 MMBtu/hr kettle is being removed from the AO accordingly. On October 10, 2024, the source confirmed that the chromate quench tank listed in DAQE -AN108330005 -12 condition II.A.13 ha s been removed from the facility and should not be referenced in the AO. The chromate quench tank is being removed from the permit.

GeneralDescription: The Dry Canyon Compressor Station, located in Carbon County, compr esses natural gas at a maximum rate of 100 MSCF/day. Natural gas pumped from well sites is routed to an inlet scrubber to remove water and then it is sent to reciprocating internal combustion engines for compression. After compression, the gas is dried by triethylene glycol dehydrators. VOC emissions from the dehydrators reboiler vents are routed through the BTEX condenser units, then to a flare for combustion. Additionally, condensate storage tank vapors are routed to the same flare.

Project_Description: Wapiti Operating, LLC (Wapiti) has requested to remove compressor engines, revise emissions limits on compressor engines, increase stack test methodologies, replace a generator engine update PTE's includ e monitoring plan at the Dry Canyon Comp ressor Station. Wapiti requested to remove CE -05 (1,680 bhp) and CE -08 (1,474 bhp) compressor engines. The emissions for the two engines were not quantified in this modification. Wapiti has requested to increase the emission limits on the existing 8 com pressor engines. The NO x emissions limit was 2.41 lb/hr ( 39.6 ppmvd) and is requested to be changed to 2.59 lb/hr (42.5 ppmvd). The CO emissions limit was 100.10 ppmvd and is requested to be changed to 135.0 ppmvd. The VOC emissions limit was 0.15 lb/hr ( 2.5 ppmvd) and is requested to be changed to 0.19 lb/hr (4.3 ppmvd). With the removal of the two compressor engines (CE -05 and CE -08), the PTE emissions increases from the emissions limit increases are balanced out. Wapiti has requested to update stack testing options into the AO to add flexibility. Wapiti has requested to replace a 98 bhp power generator engine with a 118 bhp power generator engine. With the addition of the new generator, Wapiti has requested an operational limitation that the two (2) generator engines (GE -01 and GE -02) shall not operate simultaneously. The worst -case emissions (118 bhp) estimates were used for the emissions estimates. Wapiti has also requested, the existing 405 bhp power generator engine NO x and CO emissions limit b e increased. Wapiti has submitted a new sitewide emissions analysis for all the proposed equipment with the existing equipment. During the sitewide emissions analysis, Wapiti included emissions from; compressor blowdown, engine start -up, truck load -out, pneumatic device, emergency shut -down testing, pigging operation, enclosed vapor combustor, and fugitive piping and equipment leaks. The newly included emissions were not taken into consideration in the initial permitting of this site, but the emissions h ave always been there and have been included in the emissions inventory submitted to UDAQ. The existing AO did not include PM 2.5 and greenhouse emissions (CO 2e) emissions. The PM 2.5 and CO 2e emissions have always been present at the site but, not been ta ken into consideration until now.

SourceName: Water Disposal Inc. - Produced Water Disposal Facility(Site ID: 15668)
GeneralDescription: Water Disposal Inc. (WDI) owns and operates a produced water treatment facility located approximately 4 miles northwest of Rooseve lt in Duchesne County. Produced water is received by truck from oil and gas operations and pumped into any of the produced water storage tanks. The produced water undergoes phase separation using various decanting tanks and centrifuges for the recovery of oil and condensates. The decanted water is routed to pre -injection tanks and pumped into the injection well on site. The oil separated from the produced water decanting process is pumped to heated mixing tanks , where the oil is mixed with coagulant and demulsifying agents. After a period of mixing , the solids and water that still remain in the oil are removed by a centrifuge. The oil is piped to heated oil storage tanks for transportation off site. Water from th e centrifuging process is pumped back to the decanting tanks , and the centrifuged solids are stored in a covered and sealed container before being transported and disposed of offsite.

GeneralDescription: Weber State University owns and operates a central heating plant located on the Weber State University campus in Ogden, Weber County. The central heating plant supplies steam that is converted to heat and hot water for nearby campus buildings. Onsite equip ment includes natural gas -fired satellite boilers and hot water heaters, a single natural gas -fired emergency engine, and multiple diesel -fired emergency engines not to exceed 8,000 combined horsepower for the campus.
Source_Classification: Located in Northern Wasatch Front O3 NAA, Salt Lake City UT PM 2.5 NAA Weber County Airs Source Size: B
Applicable_Federal_Standards: NSPS (Part 60), A: General Provisions NSPS (Part 60), Dc: Standards of Performance for Small Industrial -Commercial -Institutional Steam Generating Units NSPS (Part 60), IIII: Standards of Performance for Stationary Compression Ignition Internal Combustion Engines MACT (Part 63), A: General Provisions DAQE -AN122800009 -25 Page 4 MACT (Part 63), ZZZZ: National Emissions Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants for Stationary Reciprocat ing Internal Combustion Engines Title V (Part 70) Area Source
Project_Description: Under Condition II.B.3.c of Approval Order DAQE -AN122800008 -24, issued December 23, 2024, Weber State University is required to "...only install ne w generator engines that are certified to meet the Tier 3 emission standards listed in 40 CFR 89.112 for the applicable size of the generator engine." In 2018, Weber State University requested to install multiple new emergency engines, such that the comb ined site -rating increased from 3,145 HP to 8,000 HP. During this modification, the BACT proposed by Weber State University was meeting Tier 3 emission standards as listed in 40 CFR 89.112 for engines installed after 2018. In the engineering notes for the 2018 modification, it was indicated that emissions from the new engines with a rating equal or less than 600 hp were calculated using Tier 3 emission standards. However, two (2) of the new engines had a rating greater than 750 hp (Ogden Chiller Plant = 755 hp, Tracy Hall = 1,409 hp). Currently, for engines manufactured after 2006 with a rating of 750 hp or more, Tier 3 engine emission standards do not exist. Additionally, the engineering comments during the 2018 modification indicate that emissions from t he two (2) engines with individual ratings greater than 750 hp were calculated using AP-42 Section 3.4 (Large St ationary Diesel and All Stationary Dual -fuel Engines), not Tier 3 emission standards. Therefore, to clarify the intent of the above -mentioned condition, the DAQ has elected to alter the wording of Condition II.B.3.c to "The owner/operator shall only inst all new generator engines that are certified to meet the Tier 3 or better emission standards listed in 40 CFR 89.112 for the applicable size of any generator engine with a rating less than 750 hp. The owner/operator shall only install new generator engines that are certified to meet the Tier 2 or better emission standards listed in 40 CFR 89.112 for the applicable size of any generator engine with a rating of 750 hp or more." No changes to equipment or operations are taking place at the Weber State Univ ersity facility. No annual or hourly limits are changing. This is an administrative correction and no emissions are increasing. Therefore, a public comment period is not required for this project.

SourceName: West Side Grading Incorporated - Standard Pugmill Asphalt Batch Plant(Site ID: 10978)
GeneralDescription: West Side Grading Inc orporated (West Side) owns and operates a standard pugmill asphalt batch plant in Delta, Millard County, Utah. The asphalt plant itself consists of a standard drum type aggregate dryer, fueled by No. 2 burner oil, and a pugmill for mixing the hot aggregate with a sphaltic cement. A two -bin feeder is used to feed cold aggregate to the dryer. Exhaust gases and dust from the pugmill and dryer first pass through a dry dust collector. Fines from the collector are returned to the dryer to help maintain the proper ratio of course to fine material in the finished asphalt. The exhaust gasses then pass through a multiple stage wet collector before exiting to the atmosphere. The wet collector consists of a venturi scrubber, a wet fan, and two sections of duct equipped with s pray bars. Water from the wet collector system is discharged to a small settling pond on site.

SourceName: WestRock Longview, LLC - Spanish Fork Box Plant(Site ID: 11789)
GeneralDescription: WestRock Longview, LLC (WestRock) operates a corrugated board and box manufacturing plant in Spanish Fork. Rolled paper is received at the facility and stored in a large warehouse. The plant produces corrugated sheets and corrugated boxes from these paper rolls. To manufacture corrugated boxes, the paper stock is run through a corrugator machine that utilizes steam from a 50.667 MMBtu/hr boiler to corrugate and bind the sheets. Corrugated sheets are further processed by the converting machines. Converting machines include three (3) die cutters, four (4) flexo -folder -gluers, and two (2) folder -gluers. In general, the material off the corrugators is fed either to a flexo -folder -gluer or to a die cutter, both of which are capable of printing the material per customer specifications. The output from the flexo -folder -gluers is ready for shipment whereas material from the die cutters is either sent out to customers as flat sheets or fed to a fold -gluer. Finished product is stored at the facility and then shipped to the customer. All scrap material generated in the corrugator and converting machines is collected in a cyclone dust collection system. The scrap material is baled and recyc led.
Project_Description: WestRock is requesting the removal of one (1) laminator (previously listed as Equipment II.A.5 in AO DAQE -AN117890019 -23) and the removal of the 60 -inch Asitrade Corrugator (previously listed as Equipment II.A.6 (b) in A O DAQE -AN117890019 -23). The rem oval of each machine will not impact the production capacity of the facility. Therefore, there is no change to production limits or emission estimates at this time.

SourceName: Western Waters Solutions, LLC - Sand Pass Ranch Disposal Facility(Site ID: 15439)
GeneralDescription: Western Water Solutions, LLC (WWS) has requested a new AO for its Sand Pass Ranch disposal facility which takes in produced water and solids from oil a nd gas operators. The facility accepts oil field wa ste that includes production water, tank bottoms, flow- back, and other oily solids. The waste is separated into water, oil, and residual solids. The water is clean ed and placed into surface ponds for evaporation. W ater entering the evaporation ponds remains until it has been evaporated completely to the atmosphere. The oil is chemically treated, heated, and when necessary, processed through a three-phase centrifuge for separation of contaminants. After this process, it is considered cleaned oil and is stored before it i s trucked off site for sale. Solids are placed in the on-site landfarm for remediation, where the soil i s treated to minimize emissions to the atmosphere.

Project_Description: Water and Other Fluids: The WWS facility accepts various types of fluids in cluding production water, flow-back from drilling operations, and tank bottom washouts. Each of these types of wastewater will be processed using differ ent equipment to remove oil and solids. Production wate r enters the process heated to an average of 110 oF and contains less than 0.3% solids and oil. Flow-ba ck water usually contains around 3% oil and a small er portion of solids. The production water and flow-ba ck water is received from transport tank trucks and pumped through a back-flushing screen pot to remove large solids and then routed to cone-bottomed receiving tanks. Initial gravity separation takes p lace in the cone-bottomed tanks. Solids in water sl urries are extracted from the cone bottoms and transferred to a landfarm. The water leaves the cone-bottomed tanks and cascades through the remainder of the rec eiving flat-bottomed tanks which also act as additional water storage to account for the surge c apacity at the facility. Additional gravity separat ion of oil from the water and solids will occur in a gun b arrel tank. Water then flows to chemical treating t anks, where flocculent is added to drop out as much of th e remaining solids and oil. Separated oil from the chemical treating tanks is routed to the heated oil treating tank battery. Tank bottoms and flow-back containing a high amount of recoverable oil is off loaded to a process trai n designated for separation of coarse materials. This liquid ranges from ambient to 200 oF and contains from 1% to 50% oil and solids. This liquid is pumped thr ough a back-flushing filter to remove solids and on to a receiving cone-bottomed tank. Solids from the bot tom of the tanks are extracted using piping and transported to a centrifuge. The overflow water-oil mixture from the receiving cone-bottomed tank is routed to the beginning of the produced water proce ss. Oil: Oil is primarily separated from the waste water by use of the gun barrel tank. The oil is routed to a heated fixed-roof tank for further processing. Additional processing includes heating the oil to a specified temperature, which enhances separation of water, so lids, and oil during processing in the three-phase centrifuge. Oil from the centrifuge is then routed to the oil sales tank. Water is routed back to the initial produced water tanks. Oil recovered from all oil c leaning processes is directly routed to an oil sale s tank, prior to being shipped off site by tanker trucks. Solids: The solids generated on site are mixed into the lan dfarm at the facility. The solids are aerated and w atered regularly to enhance biodegradation and minimize em ission to the atmosphere. Solids collected from th e oil separation process are routed to a centrifuge f or de-watering before being transported to the land farm for final treatment.
